proposals.title: Bridging the Digital Divide in Survivorship Care: A Low-Burden, SMS-Centric Framework for Passive Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD) Collection from AYA Cancer Survivors
proposals.motivation: Adolescent and young adult (AYA) cancer survivors face significant long-term health risks requiring continuous monitoring, yet many lack reliable smartphone or broadband access, creating a critical digital divide in care. Existing telemedicine and passive sensing tools predominantly assume high-end mobile ownership, systematically excluding this vulnerable population and exacerbating health inequities. This work addresses the urgent need to design inclusive, low-cost health technologies that function within severe resource constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, SMS-based interaction framework, augmented by ultra-low-power Bluetooth beacons in clinical and community settings, can passively generate meaningful PGHD proxies (e.g., location patterns, routine adherence) without a smartphone, and that this data will be clinically actionable for supporting AYA survivor mental and physical health.
proposals.method: We will co-design with AYA survivors and clinicians a suite of SMS 'nudges' and structured queries (e.g., mood check-ins, symptom reports) sent to basic mobile phones. Concurrently, we will deploy a network of low-cost, solar-powered Bluetooth beacons in partner clinics and community centers to detect participant-carried key fob receivers, generating passive location traces. Data fusion will use a rule-based inference engine (e.g., IF beacon_A detected AND no symptom SMS reply THEN flag for follow-up) to create composite adherence and well-being indicators. The system will be evaluated via a 3-month mixed-methods field study measuring engagement, data utility for clinical decision support, and perceived burden.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, minimal-technology architecture for equitable PGHD collection, moving beyond smartphone-centric models. It will yield design principles for 'frictionless' engagement in low-resource settings and provide empirical evidence on the clinical validity of ultra-low-cost passive sensing proxies for mental health and care adherence monitoring in underserved populations.
proposals.novelty: While SMS for health (mHealth) exists, its integration with a passive, beacon-based sensing layer to create a composite PGHD stream for clinical mental healthcare is novel. This approach inverts the standard paradigm by designing for the constraints of basic phones first, rather than adapting smartphone apps downward.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Survivorship Care Plans with AYA Cancer Survivors: Integrating Passive Sensing and Patient-Generated Health Data for Actionable Clinical Support
proposals.motivation: Adolescent and young adult (AYA) cancer survivors face complex, long-term health needs, yet standard survivorship care plans (SCPs) are often static documents that fail to integrate real-world, patient-generated health data (PGHD). This gap limits the plans' relevance and utility for both patients and clinicians in managing late effects and mental health challenges. Pre-2026, the potential of passive sensing and PGHD to inform dynamic, personalized care remains underexplored in survivorship, particularly through the lens of patient-centered design.
proposals.hypothesis: That engaging AYA survivors in participatory design workshops will yield novel SCP and app interface designs which, by foregrounding the integration of passive sensing data (e.g., activity, sleep, social interaction) with active PGHD, will be perceived as more actionable and supportive of mental health and clinical communication than current standard SCP formats.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of structured participatory design workshops with 15-20 AYA survivors. Workshops will employ human-computer interaction co-design methods, including scenario-based design using data personas derived from pre-2026 passive sensing literature, and privacy card exercises adapted from Estrin's prior work to surface concerns about data sharing. Participants will iteratively prototype low-fidelity and high-fidelity mobile app interfaces that visualize synthesized sensor and self-reported data, explicitly designing for features that support clinical encounters and mental health self-management within a telemedicine context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, co-designed framework for dynamic, data-informed survivorship care plans, moving beyond static documentation. It will yield concrete design principles and interface prototypes that demonstrate how to translate passive sensing streams into clinically actionable insights and patient-facing support, specifically for the AYA population's mental and long-term care needs.
proposals.novelty: While co-design is used in healthcare, its application to integrate *passive sensing* data directly into the structure and interface of a survivorship care plan is novel. The approach uniquely bridges Estrin's focus on actionable sensing for clinical mental healthcare with the participatory, privacy-conscious design of patient-facing tools for a vulnerable, understudied population.

proposals.title: The Clinician's Lens: A Dashboard for Actionable Summarization of Patient-Generated Health Data in Mental Healthcare
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of patient-facing apps for passive sensing and symptom tracking generates rich patient-generated health data (PGHD), but clinicians are often overwhelmed by raw data streams, lacking tools to efficiently interpret them. This creates a gap between data collection and clinical utility, particularly in mental healthcare where timely, context-aware insights are critical for intervention. A clinician-facing dashboard that transforms PGHD into actionable summaries is needed to close this loop and support clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: A dashboard that applies computational summarization and anomaly detection to multi-modal PGHD (e.g., mobility, communication, app usage) will increase clinicians' perceived utility of the data and support more timely, personalized care decisions compared to reviewing raw data logs.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based dashboard that ingests data from established sensing frameworks (e.g., Beiwe, AWARE). It will implement a pipeline featuring: 1) time-series segmentation using change-point detection (e.g., PELT algorithm) to identify behavioral phases, 2) multi-modal fusion to create composite 'behavioral signatures,' and 3) anomaly scoring based on deviations from a patient's personalized baseline. The interface will present summarized timelines, anomaly alerts with context, and trend visualizations, designed through iterative co-design sessions with clinical psychologists and primary care providers.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, open-source tool for making PGHD clinically actionable, moving 'beyond detection' to support clinical workflow. It will yield design principles for clinician-facing PGHD interfaces and empirical findings on how summarized passive data influences clinical assessment and communication in telemedicine settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on patient-facing apps or detecting specific conditions, this inverts the paradigm to prioritize clinician needs, applying data summarization and anomaly detection specifically for clinical triage and consultation, rather than for automated diagnosis or patient reflection.

proposals.title: Ambient Sensing for Clinical Problem Formulation: A Framework to Infer Patient Presenting Problems from Passive Behavioral Data
proposals.motivation: In clinical mental healthcare, particularly in telemedicine, clinicians often lack the rich, contextual data needed to accurately formulate a patient's core presenting problem from brief, self-reported encounters. This can lead to misaligned treatment plans. Passive, patient-generated health data (PGHD) from smartphones and wearables offers a continuous, ecologically valid signal of behavior and context, but current sensing research focuses on detecting symptoms rather than supporting clinical problem formulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational framework that models and clusters longitudinal, multi-modal passive sensor data (e.g., mobility, communication, app use) can surface interpretable behavioral phenotypes that map to clinically meaningful presenting problem categories, thereby augmenting a clinician's diagnostic assessment.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will extract interpretable features (e.g., weekly routine fragmentation, social interaction volatility, sleep regularity) from smartphone sensor streams using established time-series analysis (e.g., FFT, entropy measures). Second, we will apply unsupervised clustering (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models or hierarchical clustering) on these features from a cohort of patients with diverse diagnoses to identify emergent behavioral clusters. Clinicians will then be engaged via 'Privacy Card'-inspired workshops to interpret these clusters, mapping them to potential presenting problems (e.g., 'social withdrawal' vs. 'agitated avoidance') and refining the feature set.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel computational framework that translates raw sensor data into clinician-interpretable problem formulations, moving 'beyond detection' to actionable assessment support, and 2) a methodological blueprint for co-designing clinical AI tools that surface algorithmic models to clinicians for critique and integration, addressing the 'illusion of empathy' in data displays.
proposals.novelty: While prior work detects specific symptoms (e.g., depression), this approach uniquely targets the higher-order clinical task of problem formulation by using unsupervised learning to reveal latent behavioral structures, which are then semantically anchored by clinicians, not predefined by researchers.

proposals.title: Designing for Trust and Control: A Configurable Voice-First Ambient Interface (VFAI) Framework for Diverse User Stances on Health Data Sharing
proposals.motivation: Voice-First Ambient Interfaces (VFAIs) offer significant potential for passive health sensing and patient-generated health data (PGHD) collection, particularly in mental healthcare and chronic disease management. However, existing designs often assume user enthusiasm for data sharing, neglecting the skepticism prevalent among many patients, especially older adults and those with stigmatized conditions. This one-size-fits-all approach risks alienating users and limiting the real-world adoption and equity of these technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: A VFAI design framework that explicitly supports user-configurable data sharing granularity and feedback transparency will increase perceived trust and acceptability among both enthusiastic and skeptical users, without degrading system utility for clinical support.
proposals.method: We will develop and evaluate a prototype VFAI system using a participatory design process with two distinct user groups: older adults with depression (skeptical-leaning) and patients with well-managed hypertension (enthusiast-leaning). The technical core will be a modular software architecture where data processing pipelines are governed by user-configured 'privacy cards'—tangible or UI representations of data-sharing rules. We will implement differential privacy techniques for audio feature extraction (e.g., for detecting social engagement or vocal stress) to allow users to select sharing levels from raw audio to fully anonymized metrics. The system will include a 'transparency dashboard' that visualizes what data is collected, inferred, and shared, drawing on principles from the 'Privacy Cards' method.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, implementable VFAI architecture supporting granular user control over data sharing, 2) empirical findings on how design configurations affect trust and acceptability across diverse user stances, and 3) concrete design guidelines for building 'actionable sensing' systems that respect patient autonomy, advancing beyond mere detection to sustainable integration in clinical mental healthcare and telemedicine.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied privacy concerns, this proposal uniquely integrates configurable privacy controls directly into the sensing and inference pipeline of a VFAI, creating a single system capable of dynamically adapting to polarized user preferences—a step beyond diagnostic studies or static privacy settings.

proposals.title: Contextualizing Care: Using In-Situ Experience Sampling to Unpack the Real-World Use and Perceptions of Voice-First Ambient Interfaces in Mental Health
proposals.motivation: Voice-First Ambient Interfaces (VFAIs) offer promising passive sensing for mental health, but their real-world adoption and therapeutic impact are poorly understood. Clinicians lack actionable insights into how patients perceive and interact with these systems in their daily lives, limiting their ability to provide effective, personalized telemedicine support. This gap hinders the translation of passive sensing data into meaningful clinical care.
proposals.hypothesis: Deploying ecological momentary assessments (EMAs) via the VFAI will reveal significant, context-dependent variations in user perceptions (e.g., trust, utility, privacy concerns) that correlate with system engagement and self-reported mental health outcomes, providing a critical link between passive data and actionable clinical insight.
proposals.method: We will deploy a micro-interaction EMA protocol on a commercial VFAI platform (e.g., Amazon Echo) used by patients with anxiety or depression in a telemedicine program. Brief, context-aware audio surveys will be triggered pseudo-randomly and by system events (e.g., after a check-in prompt). We will use multilevel modeling to analyze the relationship between EMA responses (perceptions, context), passive engagement logs, and weekly Patient-Reported Outcome Measures (PROMs), identifying key contextual predictors of sustained use and perceived benefit.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodology for capturing in-situ PGHD (patient-generated health data) that blends passive sensing with active self-report. It will yield concrete design guidelines for VFAIs that support longitudinal engagement in mental healthcare. The findings will advance the field beyond detection towards actionable sensing by providing clinicians with interpretable models of patient-system interaction.
proposals.novelty: While EMAs are common in mobile health, their integration into a voice-first, ambient interface for *in-situ* capture of perceptions *about the system itself* is novel. This moves beyond evaluating system performance in labs to understanding the lived experience of therapeutic technology, directly addressing the 'illusion of empathy' critique in HCI.

proposals.title: Dual-Care: A Voice-First Conversational Agent for Caregiver Task Management and Stress Mitigation
proposals.motivation: Informal caregivers, especially for older adults with cognitive or mental health conditions, face immense chronic stress and burden, leading to adverse health outcomes. While patient-generated health data (PGHD) and telemedicine tools focus on the patient, there is a critical gap in passive, ambient technologies designed to directly support the caregiver's own workflow and well-being within the home. This work addresses the dual challenge of caregiving by creating tools for the caregiver themselves.
proposals.hypothesis: A voice-first conversational agent, designed with explicit privacy and empathy displays, can reduce caregiver burden and perceived stress by reliably supporting task management and providing a low-friction channel for logging observations, compared to standard digital note-taking methods.
proposals.method: We will design and deploy 'Dual-Care', a voice-agent built on open-source platforms like Mycroft or Rhasspy for privacy. It will use a hybrid architecture: 1) a rule-based dialog manager for structured task logging (medication, appointments) using pre-2026 intent classification (e.g., Rasa NLU), and 2) an LSTM-based emotion recognition model (trained on open datasets like IEMOCAP) to detect caregiver vocal stress and trigger supportive, non-clinical responses. The system will be evaluated through a 4-week mixed-methods study with 20 caregiver-patient dyads, comparing stress metrics (PSS) and usability against a control journaling app.
proposals.contribution: This contributes a novel framework for 'actionable sensing' in caregiver support, translating passive voice interaction into concrete task support. It advances human-computer interaction by critically examining how emotion displays in voice agents can be designed to avoid the 'illusion of empathy' while providing genuine utility, and it extends the PGHD paradigm to include caregiver-generated data.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior voice health interfaces focused on patient monitoring, this system explicitly targets the caregiver's dual role, using voice not for detection but for actionable support. It integrates privacy-sensitive design (inspired by 'Privacy Cards') with real-time, non-invasive stress sensing specifically for the caregiver context.

proposals.title: Clinician-as-Consumer: Designing Patient-First Self-Management Apps with Actionable Passive Data Summaries for Clinical Mental Healthcare
proposals.motivation: Patient-generated health data (PGHD) from mobile and passive sensing holds promise for chronic care, but apps are often designed for clinician workflows, creating burdensome data streams and undermining patient agency. This is particularly problematic in mental health, where engagement is fragile and data is sensitive. A patient-first design paradigm is needed to support self-management while effectively translating patient-centered data into clinically actionable insights.
proposals.hypothesis: That a mobile health app designed explicitly for patient self-management, which surfaces passive sensing data (e.g., mobility, communication, sleep) through patient-facing reflective interfaces, will yield higher engagement and more meaningful patient-generated context, enabling the generation of automated, privacy-sensitive summaries that clinicians find more actionable than raw data streams.
proposals.method: We will develop a smartphone app using the Beiwe platform for passive sensing (GPS, accelerometer, usage logs) and active ecological momentary assessments (EMAs). The core technical innovation is a two-layer data processing pipeline: 1) A patient-facing 'nudge engine' using simple anomaly detection (e.g., isolation from communication patterns) to trigger self-reflection prompts, and 2) A clinician-facing summary generator that uses interpretable rule-based clustering (e.g., k-means on weekly behavioral fingerprints) and natural language generation templates to convert a week of raw data into a narrative summary of 'patterns and exceptions,' co-designed with clinicians. Evaluation will involve a 12-week deployment with 30 patient-clinician dyads in a telemental health setting, measuring engagement, utility, and perceived empathy via mixed methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel app architecture and design principles for 'patient-first, clinician-informed' digital health tools. It will advance the field of actionable sensing by demonstrating how to transform passive data into patient-curated, clinically useful narratives, moving beyond mere detection to supporting patient-clinician collaboration. It will also provide empirical evidence on how such designs impact therapeutic alliance in telemedicine.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses passive data for clinician alerts or patient dashboards, this inverts the paradigm by making the patient the primary user of sensed data for self-management, treating the clinician as a consumer of synthesized, patient-contextualized summaries—a shift aligned with participatory healthcare but technically unexplored for passive sensing in clinical mental health.

proposals.title: Designing for Clinical Integration: A Framework for Patient-Generated Health Data Apps as Structured Visit Supplements
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of health apps often positions them as cost-saving replacements for clinical care, risking fragmented care and clinician burden. This is especially problematic in mental health and chronic disease management, where nuanced, longitudinal patient context is critical. Grounded in pre-2026 concerns about telemedicine scalability and the need for actionable PGHD, this research addresses the design gap for apps that explicitly structure data to augment, not bypass, the clinician-patient relationship.
proposals.hypothesis: That health apps designed with explicit 'clinical handoff' mechanisms—which structure patient-generated data to directly prepare for and follow up on in-person visits—will improve perceived clinical utility and shared decision-making, compared to apps designed for standalone self-management.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, iterative design research approach. First, we will conduct participatory design workshops with clinicians and patients to co-create 'visit-centric' app prototypes that feature structured data capture prompts (e.g., pre-visit question builders, post-visit plan trackers) and summary dashboards for clinical review. These prototypes will be evaluated in a longitudinal field deployment using a comparative study design, measuring outcomes such as clinician efficiency, patient preparedness for visits, and alliance, analyzed via pre-2026 statistical methods (e.g., mixed-effects models) and thematic analysis of interview data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel design framework and concrete interaction patterns for 'visit-supplement' apps, moving beyond detection to structured clinical action. It will provide empirical evidence on how to make PGHD more interpretable and actionable within existing clinical workflows, thereby advancing the field of clinically integrated sensing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on data collection or remote monitoring, this proposal uniquely centers the design of the app's output—the clinical handoff—as the primary intervention. It directly challenges the replacement paradigm by making the in-person visit the central organizing principle of the app's architecture, a non-obvious shift from standalone self-care or telemedicine models.

proposals.title: Sustaining Engagement in Chronic Care: A Longitudinal Study of Adaptive Personalization and Clinician-Mediated Feedback in IBD Self-Management Apps
proposals.motivation: For patients with chronic conditions like Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD), long-term engagement with self-management apps is critical for generating meaningful Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD) and improving outcomes. However, app engagement typically declines sharply after initial use, undermining their clinical utility and the potential for longitudinal data collection. This problem persists despite the proliferation of telemedicine and digital health tools, creating a gap between passive sensing capabilities and sustained patient support.
proposals.hypothesis: An IBD self-management app that combines adaptive, context-aware personalization of data collection prompts with a structured, clinician-mediated feedback loop will significantly improve long-term patient engagement and adherence over a 12-month period compared to standard static apps.
proposals.method: We will develop and deploy a mobile app for a 12-month randomized controlled trial with IBD patients. The intervention app will use a reinforcement learning (RL) agent (e.g., a contextual bandit algorithm) to adapt the timing, frequency, and content of ecological momentary assessments (EMAs) and symptom logging prompts based on user context (e.g., time, location, recent activity) and prior engagement patterns. This passive, adaptive layer will be integrated with an active, clinician-facing dashboard where care team members can review summarized PGHD trends and send periodic, personalized text-based feedback or encouragement, creating a 'clinician-in-the-loop' support system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated technical framework for adaptive engagement in chronic care apps, moving beyond simple reminders. It will provide empirical evidence on the combined effect of algorithmic personalization and human-clinician support on long-term adherence. The findings will offer concrete design principles for creating 'actionable sensing' systems that sustain patient participation to yield clinically useful longitudinal data.
proposals.novelty: While personalization and clinician involvement are known concepts, their integration into a unified, longitudinal system for IBD care, using an RL agent to optimize patient burden and engagement dynamically, is novel. This approach directly addresses the 'illusion of empathy' in HCI by grounding system adaptation in both algorithmic context-sensing and genuine, periodic human-clinician interaction.

proposals.title: Beyond the Screen: Validating and Augmenting Mobile App-Based Mental Health Assessments with Passive Physiological Sensing
proposals.motivation: Mobile apps and patient-generated health data (PGHD) are increasingly used for remote mental health monitoring, but reliance on app-based tasks (e.g., surveys, active logging) is insufficient. These methods are prone to self-report bias, user burden, and fail to capture critical, objective physiological correlates of mental state. This gap limits the clinical actionability of digital tools, a key concern in telemedicine and clinical support systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating passive, continuous physiological sensing (e.g., from wearables) with active, app-based behavioral tasks will yield a more valid, reliable, and clinically actionable assessment of mental health status than app-based data alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a multimodal sensing platform for Android/iOS that concurrently collects: (1) active app-based tasks (ecological momentary assessment, cognitive tests) and (2) passive physiological data (heart rate variability, electrodermal activity, sleep patterns) via a research-grade wearable (e.g., Empatica E4). A co-design phase with clinicians will define actionable sensing targets. We will use time-series fusion algorithms (e.g., multimodal deep learning with LSTM networks) to model the relationship between these data streams and validated clinical scales in a 4-week observational study with patients managing anxiety/depression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework and evidence for when and how passive sensing complements active PGHD, moving 'beyond detection' towards integrated assessment. It will yield design principles for creating more empathetic and effective human-computer interaction in clinical mental healthcare by making digital tools more responsive to unspoken physiological states.
proposals.novelty: While multimodal sensing exists, this work uniquely challenges the sufficiency of the app-as-instrument model by rigorously testing the added clinical value of passive data within a real-world care support context, directly addressing the 'illusion of empathy' in digital health interfaces.

proposals.title: Bridging the Actionability Gap: Integrating Ecological Momentary Assessment with Passive Sensing for Contextualized Mental Health Feedback
proposals.motivation: In mental health, passive sensing data (PGHD) can detect behavioral shifts, but often lacks the contextual, subjective meaning needed for actionable clinical insight. This creates a gap between detection and effective intervention, particularly in telemedicine and supportive care settings where clinician time is limited. Rich, in-the-moment qualitative feedback is crucial to interpret sensor data and surface patient concerns, aligning with the move towards more empathetic and participatory human-computer interaction in healthcare.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating structured, micro-interaction Experience Sampling Methods (ESM) from HCI into a passive sensing app will generate contextualized, interpretable data that improves the clinical utility of PGHD and supports more patient-centered care planning.
proposals.method: We will develop and deploy a mobile app that triggers brief, context-aware ESM prompts based on real-time analysis of passive sensor streams (e.g., location, phone use, actigraphy). Using algorithms for anomaly detection (e.g., isolation forests on behavioral features) and routine modeling, the system will solicit qualitative feedback at moments of detected behavioral deviation or low activity. The interface will employ HCI-informed, low-burden interaction patterns (e.g., adaptive questioning, emotion wheels) to capture mood, stress, and social context, linking this self-report directly to the sensor-derived trigger.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodological framework for generating 'context-annotated' PGHD, moving beyond detection towards explainable sensing. It will contribute concrete design principles for integrating active and passive sensing in clinical mental health support tools, ultimately aiming to enhance shared decision-making between patients and clinicians.
proposals.novelty: While ESM and passive sensing are both established, their deep integration for *algorithmically-triggered*, context-sensitive probing to interpret sensor anomalies in real-world clinical care is non-obvious. This directly addresses the 'illusion of empathy' in HCI by creating a structured channel for patient voice within automated sensing systems.

proposals.title: A Stepped-Wedge Pragmatic Trial to Evaluate the Implementation and Effectiveness of a Patient-Generated Health Data App for Chronic Low Back Pain in Primary Care
proposals.motivation: Chronic low back pain (CLBP) is a leading cause of disability, often managed in primary care where time and resources for sustained behavioral support are limited. Patient-generated health data (PGHD) from apps like Limbr offer promise for passive sensing and self-management, but their real-world clinical effectiveness and implementation pathways remain poorly understood, creating a gap between digital tool development and actionable clinical support.
proposals.hypothesis: A stepped-wedge implementation of the Limbr app in primary care clinics will lead to a statistically significant and clinically meaningful reduction in patient-reported pain interference compared to standard care alone, and the design will surface key, actionable barriers and facilitators to integrating PGHD into clinical workflows.
proposals.method: We will adapt the public health stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial design. Approximately 24 primary care clinics (clusters) will be randomized to one of 6 sequential crossover points. All clinics begin in the control phase (standard care). At each step, a new group of clinics crosses over to the intervention (standard care + Limbr app), which includes passive activity sensing, patient-reported outcomes, and clinician-facing dashboards. The primary outcome is change in PROMIS Pain Interference score. Mixed-effects models will control for time and cluster effects. Qualitative analysis of clinician interviews and app use logs will identify implementation factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, real-world evidence for the effectiveness of a PGHD app for CLBP. It will contribute a methodological framework for evaluating digital health interventions in clinical settings that balances rigor with pragmatic implementation needs. The findings will yield concrete design and policy insights for integrating passive sensing and PGHD into telemedicine and clinical support workflows for chronic conditions.
proposals.novelty: While stepped-wedge designs are established in public health, their application to evaluate a consumer-grade PGHD app within a clinical care continuum is novel. This approach moves beyond detection or feasibility studies to directly test how actionable sensing translates into clinical mental and physical health outcomes in routine practice.

proposals.title: Predicting Long-Term Engagement with Passive Sensing and PGHD for Chronic Mental Health Management
proposals.motivation: Digital health interventions, particularly for chronic mental health conditions, suffer from steep declines in engagement after initial pilot periods, limiting their clinical utility and the value of patient-generated health data (PGHD). While short-term feasibility studies are common, there is a critical lack of understanding of the patient-specific factors that predict sustained, long-term use of passive sensing and telemedicine tools in real-world care settings. This gap hinders the design of systems that can effectively support clinicians and patients over the longitudinal course of care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of baseline patient characteristics (e.g., clinical severity, digital literacy, social support) and early engagement patterns (e.g., consistency of app use, reactivity to notifications) with a mental health support platform will predict long-term (6+ month) adherence and clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal observational study deploying a smartphone-based platform for passive sensing (location, mobility, communication) and active PGHD collection (ecological momentary assessments) with patients in ongoing clinical mental healthcare. We will apply survival analysis (Cox proportional hazards models) to model time-to-disengagement, and use machine learning techniques (e.g., Random Forests, Gradient Boosting) on feature vectors derived from the first 30 days of use to classify subsequent 6-month adherence. The analysis will integrate pre-2026 clinical scales and privacy concern measures surfaced via methods like Privacy Cards.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated model for predicting long-term engagement failure, enabling proactive clinical support interventions. It will advance the field of actionable sensing by shifting focus from pure detection to sustaining therapeutic engagement, and provide concrete design guidelines for creating mental health technologies that support enduring patient-clinician collaboration.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond the standard 3-month pilot paradigm to study engagement in an ongoing clinical context, and uniquely integrates pre-2026 methods for surfacing patient mental models (e.g., of privacy) as predictive features alongside sensor and clinical data.

proposals.title: From Passive Sensing to Actionable Notes: Designing Clinician-Facing EHR Summaries for Mental Health
proposals.motivation: Patient-generated health data (PGHD) from passive sensing apps holds promise for mental healthcare, but it risks overwhelming clinicians with raw data streams. Pre-2026, integration into clinical workflows and Electronic Health Records (EHRs) is a critical barrier; data must be synthesized to support, not disrupt, clinical decision-making. This is especially urgent in telemedicine and collaborative care models where efficient, empathetic data presentation is key.
proposals.hypothesis: That clinician-facing EHR summaries, which transform passive sensing data into narrative-style progress notes highlighting salient behavioral trends and potential risks, will improve perceived utility and reduce cognitive load compared to raw data visualizations, thereby increasing the likelihood of clinical action.
proposals.method: We will co-design prototype EHR summary interfaces with clinicians using participatory methods. The technical approach will involve developing a pipeline that ingests multi-modal passive data (e.g., mobility, communication patterns) and applies interpretable time-series segmentation (e.g., change-point detection) and anomaly detection algorithms to identify clinically meaningful periods. A rule-based narrative generator will synthesize these periods into a structured clinical note format, emphasizing changes from baseline. We will evaluate prototypes through simulated clinical scenarios, measuring perceived utility, cognitive load, and decision confidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel design framework and prototype system for integrating PGHD into EHRs as actionable clinical notes, moving 'beyond detection.' It will yield empirical evidence on how data presentation influences clinical utility and provide concrete design guidelines for human-computer interaction in clinical mental health settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work surfaces data or privacy concerns, this proposal uniquely focuses on the EHR integration point, synthesizing raw sensor data into a clinician-familiar narrative format. It bridges the gap between actionable sensing research and the pragmatic constraints of clinical documentation and decision-making workflows.

proposals.title: Scaling Digital Health Coaching: A Group-Based Model for Managing Chronic Conditions Using Passive Sensing and Asynchronous Support
proposals.motivation: The scalability of personalized digital health interventions is limited by the 1:1 clinician-to-patient model, creating access barriers and high costs. Group-based virtual care, common in mental health, remains underexplored for managing physical chronic conditions (e.g., diabetes, hypertension) using patient-generated health data (PGHD). This research addresses the critical need for sustainable, effective models that extend clinical support beyond individual telemedicine sessions.
proposals.hypothesis: A health coach-led, group-based virtual intervention, augmented by passive sensing and asynchronous communication, will yield non-inferior clinical outcomes and higher patient engagement compared to a standard 1:1 telemedicine model, while improving coach efficiency and scalability.
proposals.method: We will develop and deploy a mobile app for a 12-week pilot with patients managing type 2 diabetes. The app will integrate passive sensing (e.g., step count, sleep via smartphone/consumer wearables) and patient-reported outcomes. A single health coach will facilitate a closed group via asynchronous text/video messages and weekly themed prompts. We will implement a clustering algorithm (k-means based on baseline PGHD and survey data) to form cohesive groups. Interaction analysis will use NLP techniques (e.g., LIWC, topic modeling) to characterize group dynamics and coach workload.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for scalable, group-based digital health coaching grounded in PGHD. It will contribute empirical evidence on the feasibility, patient engagement, and clinical outcomes of this model, and offer design principles for balancing group cohesion with individual needs in asynchronous care settings.
proposals.novelty: While group therapy exists, applying a group coaching model to chronic physical disease management via passive sensing and asynchronous support is novel. It moves beyond detection-focused sensing to create an actionable, scalable support system that redefines the clinician's role from direct data monitor to group facilitator.

proposals.title: Privacy Cards and the Data Pipeline: Eliciting Patient Mental Models for Sharing Passive PGHD in Clinical Mental Healthcare
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of passive sensing for Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD) offers unprecedented opportunities for proactive mental healthcare, yet its clinical integration remains limited. A critical barrier is the lack of understanding of patient perspectives on sharing this intimate, continuous data stream with clinicians, which risks creating systems that are technically sound but ethically and practically misaligned with user needs and concerns.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients' willingness to share passive PGHD is not a binary decision but is shaped by nuanced mental models of the data pipeline, where specific attributes of data (e.g., type, granularity, inferred state), context of use, and perceived clinician empathy act as key decision points.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Privacy Cards methodology, previously used for ambient interfaces, to create a novel card-sorting and scenario-based interview protocol. Participants (n=30-40 patients with depression/anxiety) will engage with cards representing data types (e.g., GPS, keystroke dynamics, sleep audio), processing stages (raw vs. inferred mood score), and sharing contexts (crisis alert vs. weekly review). Using thematic analysis of interview transcripts, we will map decision frameworks and identify 'red lines' for sharing. The study will be embedded within an existing telemedicine mental health support platform to ensure ecological validity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a structured taxonomy of patient concerns and preferences specific to passive PGHD sharing, moving beyond generic privacy discussions. It will provide actionable design guidelines for clinical decision support tools that incorporate passive sensing, ensuring they are co-designed with patient agency and contextual integrity at their core.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines privacy for active PGHD or generic IoT data, this study uniquely applies a mental models framework to the specific, continuous, and inferential nature of passive clinical sensing, bridging Estrin's work on actionable sensing with her recent focus on emotion displays and empathic interfaces in HCI.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Shared Data Review Practices: A Participatory Framework for Clinician-Patient Dyads in Mental Health Telemedicine
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of patient-generated health data (PGHD) from passive sensing and self-tracking in mental healthcare creates a critical gap: clinicians and patients lack structured, mutually understood practices for reviewing this data collaboratively. This leads to misinterpretation, misaligned expectations, and undermines the therapeutic alliance, a cornerstone of effective care. Current telemedicine platforms often treat data review as a one-way transmission from patient to clinician, failing to leverage the dyadic relationship.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured participatory design process with clinician-patient dyads will yield novel, actionable shared data review practices which improve perceived empathy, alignment on treatment goals, and the clinical utility of PGHD compared to standard, clinician-centric review protocols.
proposals.method: We will recruit 10-12 existing clinician-patient dyads from a community mental health telemedicine service. Using a series of three iterative, facilitated co-design sessions per dyad, we will employ methods like 'Privacy Card' workshops to surface mental models of data sharing and review, and scenario-based prototyping of data dashboards. Sessions will be recorded, transcribed, and analyzed using thematic analysis to identify core requirements and tensions. These findings will be synthesized into a lightweight, modular interaction framework specifying roles, rituals, and interface components for shared review, which will be implemented as a prototype plugin for an existing telemedicine platform (e.g., Zoom) using standard web technologies (JavaScript, WebSockets) for real-time, annotated data exploration.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel methodological approach for dyad-centric design in health informatics, 2) a concrete, empirically-derived framework of shared data review practices, and 3) open-source prototype software demonstrating how to embed these practices into clinical telemedicine workflows, moving beyond passive detection towards actionable, relationship-centered sensing.
proposals.novelty: While participatory design is common in HCI, applying it to the intact clinician-patient dyad unit to design *interaction practices* (not just tools) for PGHD review is novel. It directly addresses the 'illusion of empathy' in data displays by co-constructing the emotional and interpretive context of data review with both parties.

proposals.title: Simulated PGHD Cases for Clinician Training: A Framework for Interpreting Passive Sensing Data in Mental Healthcare
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of passive patient-generated health data (PGHD) from smartphones and wearables presents a novel challenge for clinicians, particularly in mental health. Clinicians lack training frameworks to interpret these continuous, often noisy, behavioral streams and translate them into actionable clinical insights. This gap risks data being ignored or misinterpreted, undermining the potential of PGHD to support proactive, personalized care.
proposals.hypothesis: That a simulation-based training platform using curated, realistic PGHD case studies will improve clinicians' accuracy in interpreting passive sensing data and increase their confidence in formulating data-informed clinical responses, compared to standard didactic training.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based simulation platform where clinicians review longitudinal PGHD traces (simulated from real-world datasets) for virtual patients with detailed clinical backgrounds. The platform will present passive data (e.g., mobility, communication, sleep patterns) alongside traditional clinical notes. Using an interactive interface, clinicians will be prompted to identify anomalies, propose hypotheses, and suggest clinical actions. We will employ algorithms from time-series analysis (e.g., change point detection, anomaly detection via Isolation Forest) to generate and label realistic synthetic PGHD anomalies within the cases. A controlled study with mental health clinicians will evaluate the platform's efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scalable training methodology for the era of passive sensing, directly addressing the clinician-in-the-loop challenge. It will contribute a publicly available repository of simulated PGHD cases for mental health and a framework for evaluating clinical data interpretation skills, moving beyond detection towards actionable use.
proposals.novelty: While clinical simulation is well-established in medicine, its application to training for passive PGHD interpretation is novel. This approach uniquely bridges the gap between technical sensing research and clinical practice by focusing on the clinician's cognitive and decision-making process.

proposals.title: From Passive Data to Proactive Care: A Real-Time Search Pattern Monitoring System for Mental Health Crisis Alerts
proposals.motivation: The rise of patient-generated health data (PGHD) offers unprecedented opportunities for proactive mental healthcare, yet current sensing research often focuses on detection without clear pathways to clinical action. Clinicians lack tools to interpret passive, longitudinal behavioral data streams, like search patterns, in real-time to identify moments of acute risk. This gap between passive sensing and actionable clinical support is a critical barrier to effective telemedicine and remote care models.
proposals.hypothesis: That real-time, privacy-sensitive analysis of search query patterns—including lexical features, temporal dynamics, and topic shifts—can generate clinically valid alerts for potential mental health crises, enabling timely, supportive interventions by a clinical care team.
proposals.method: We will develop a system architecture that ingests anonymized search logs via a local client, applying differential privacy techniques at the edge. A real-time processing pipeline will extract features (e.g., sentiment via LIWC-22 dictionaries, query repetition, late-night activity spikes) and apply a lightweight anomaly detection algorithm (Isolation Forest) against personalized baselines. Alerts, surfaced via a clinician dashboard co-designed with mental health professionals, will include interpretable context (e.g., "sustained increase in hopelessness-related queries") to support clinical decision-making, not just binary flags.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a functional prototype demonstrating how passive PGHD can be transformed into actionable clinical intelligence. It will advance the field of 'actionable sensing' by providing a concrete framework for real-time alert generation and a co-design model for clinician-in-the-loop systems, moving beyond pure detection to support intervention.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on retrospective analysis of social media for mental health, this system focuses on real-time, privacy-preserving analysis of a ubiquitous but understudied data stream (search patterns) and is explicitly designed to integrate into clinical workflows, addressing the 'illusion of empathy' critique by prioritizing actionable context over raw emotion display.

proposals.title: Passive Sensing of Functional Mobility as a Primary Outcome: Validating Patient-Generated Health Data Against Self-Report and Clinical Engagement in Chronic Care
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of passive sensing for patient-generated health data (PGHD) has created a need to identify clinically meaningful digital biomarkers. While patient-reported outcomes (PROs) and engagement metrics are common, they can be burdensome or misaligned with functional health. Functional mobility, a core indicator of independence and health status in chronic conditions, can be passively and continuously measured via smartphones and wearables, offering a potential objective primary outcome for remote care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal, passively sensed functional mobility metrics will correlate more strongly with clinician-assessed health status and predict adverse health events better than self-reported health status or application engagement metrics alone in a remote chronic care management program.
proposals.method: We will deploy a smartphone-based sensing platform (e.g., Beiwe, AWARE framework) to a cohort of 150 patients with chronic cardiopulmonary conditions in a telemedicine program. We will extract established mobility features (GPS circularity, location variance, step count regularity) and novel interaction-derived mobility proxies. Using mixed-effects models and time-series analysis, we will compare the predictive power of these passive features against weekly electronic PROs and app engagement logs for forecasting clinician-rated health deteriorations (via bi-weekly tele-visits) and hospital readmissions over six months.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for selecting primary outcomes in PGHD research, moving beyond detection to actionable sensing. It will contribute a validated framework for using passive functional mobility as a core digital biomarker in clinical mental and physical healthcare, directly supporting clinician decision-making in remote care models.
proposals.novelty: This study directly challenges the assumption that self-report or engagement are the most meaningful digital endpoints, proposing instead that passively quantified real-world function is a superior and more objective primary outcome. It integrates Estrin's focus on passive sensing for health with her recent critical work on actionable clinical tools and the complexities of patient-clinician-computer interaction.

proposals.title: Assessing and Augmenting Digital Readiness: A Pre-Intervention Framework for Inclusive mHealth Engagement with Older Adults
proposals.motivation: mHealth tools hold promise for passive sensing and patient-generated health data (PGHD) to support older adult care, but digital literacy barriers can exclude those most in need, creating inequitable access and biased research cohorts. Pre-2026, interventions often assume baseline competency, leading to high attrition and failed deployments. A systematic, person-centered approach to pre-enrollment assessment and training is needed to ensure inclusive participation and valid data collection.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, low-burden digital literacy screening and modular training program, delivered via telemedicine support channels, will significantly increase the proportion of enrolled older adults who can successfully use a target mHealth intervention for passive mental health monitoring.
proposals.method: We will co-design with clinicians and older adults a two-stage framework: 1) A screening tool using a tablet-based interactive assessment (e.g., completing mock tasks on a simulated app interface) to profile competencies relevant to target mHealth use (e.g., notification response, passive data consent). 2) A just-in-time, adaptive training program delivered via a clinician-supported telemedicine platform, using branching video tutorials and practice scenarios based on screening results. Success will be measured by task completion rates and system interaction logs post-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, open-source protocol for digital readiness screening and augmentation, moving beyond simple detection of barriers to actionable support. It will contribute to methods for equitable recruitment in PGHD research and offer clinical telemedicine teams a structured tool to prepare patients for digital health interventions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic digital literacy measures, our approach is intervention-specific and co-designed to surface mental models of privacy and interaction, as explored in Estrin's prior work on ambient interfaces. It integrates screening directly into the clinical onboarding workflow to create actionable, personalized training pathways rather than serving as a mere exclusion filter.

proposals.title: Characterizing Engagement Archetypes and Predictors in mHealth Cardiac Rehabilitation: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Patient-Generated Health Data
proposals.motivation: Mobile health (mHealth) platforms for cardiac rehabilitation (CR) show promise for improving outcomes, but engagement is often suboptimal and heterogeneous. Understanding the distinct patterns of use and the patient characteristics—including psychosocial factors and digital literacy—that predict them is critical for designing equitable, supportive, and effective interventions. This addresses a key gap in moving from passive sensing and detection to actionable, personalized clinical support in chronic care management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that participants in an mHealth-CR intervention will cluster into distinct, interpretable engagement archetypes (e.g., sustained, declining, sporadic, disengaged) and that these archetypes can be predicted by a combination of baseline clinical, sociodemographic, and technology-related characteristics.
proposals.method: We will analyze PGHD (app logins, symptom checks, educational module completion, step counts) from a 12-week mHealth-CR trial. Using time-series clustering (Dynamic Time Warping with k-means) and sequence analysis (TraMineR), we will identify engagement trajectories. We will then apply multinomial logistic regression and classification trees (CART) to model archetype membership against baseline survey data (clinical status, PHQ-8/GAD-7, digital self-efficacy, social support) and demographic variables. Semi-structured interviews with purposively sampled participants from each archetype will contextualize quantitative patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated typology of mHealth-CR engagement, moving beyond simple adherence metrics. It will identify actionable predictors of engagement patterns, enabling clinicians to proactively tailor support. The findings will contribute to the design of more adaptive, empathetic telemedicine systems that bridge the gap between clinical utility and sustained patient use.
proposals.novelty: While prior work often correlates single factors with adherence, this study integrates rich PGHD with psychosocial and digital literacy variables to model multi-faceted engagement trajectories as the primary outcome. It applies Estrin's focus on actionable sensing and empathetic HCI to the specific challenge of sustaining engagement in chronic care, moving from detection to characterization for clinical support.

proposals.title: Feature-Level Efficacy in mHealth: A Randomized, Multi-Arm Trial of Feedback Mechanisms for Patient-Generated Health Data in Chronic Disease Management
proposals.motivation: mHealth platforms are proliferating, but their design is often driven by technological capability rather than evidence of which specific software features effectively support patients and clinicians. This leads to wasted development effort and suboptimal clinical integration, particularly for managing chronic conditions where sustained engagement is critical. Understanding the causal impact of discrete feedback features—like visualizations, alerts, or summary reports—on both patient adherence and clinical decision-making remains a significant gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that feedback mechanisms which align with clinicians' mental models of disease progression and provide actionable, time-bound summaries will lead to higher patient engagement and more clinically meaningful use of patient-generated health data (PGHD) than mechanisms focused solely on patient self-monitoring or raw data presentation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month, multi-arm randomized controlled trial with patients managing type 2 diabetes, using a modular mHealth platform built on the Beiwe research framework. Participants will be randomized to different feature sets: 1) raw data stream, 2) patient-facing visualizations/gamification, 3) clinician-facing automated summary reports with flagging, and 4) a shared dashboard with negotiated goal-setting. Engagement will be measured via platform logs and ecological momentary assessments. Clinical integration will be assessed through mixed-methods analysis of clinician interviews and EHR audit logs, using thematic analysis and multivariate regression to isolate feature-level effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a feature-level efficacy map for mHealth design, moving beyond platform-level comparisons. It will yield concrete design guidelines for creating 'actionable sensing' systems that bridge patient self-care and clinical workflows. The findings will directly inform how to operationalize PGHD in telemedicine and collaborative care models, particularly for mental and chronic physical health comorbidities.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares whole apps or studies general engagement, this proposal isolates and tests specific feedback mechanisms as independent variables, linking them to dual outcomes of patient behavior and clinician utility. It applies Estrin's concepts of 'privacy cards' and 'illusion of empathy' to critically evaluate how feature design influences trust and action in clinical dyads.

proposals.title: A Framework for Scaling mHealth-Enabled Care Redesign: Implementation and Equity Considerations for a National Telehealth Service
proposals.motivation: The rapid expansion of telehealth, accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic, has created an urgent need for systematic frameworks to integrate patient-generated health data (PGHD) into scalable, equitable, and clinically effective care pathways. Current mHealth research often focuses on small-scale efficacy studies, leaving a critical gap in understanding how to operationalize these tools within complex national healthcare systems to support diverse patient populations, particularly in chronic disease and mental health management.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured implementation framework that co-designs clinical workflows with frontline providers and embeds privacy-sensitive, actionable PGHD analytics will enable the successful, equitable scaling of mHealth-enabled care redesign (mHealth-CR) as a national telehealth service, improving access and outcomes while maintaining clinician support.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, iterative design process. First, we will conduct ethnographic studies and Privacy Card workshops with clinicians and patients from diverse health systems to model workflows and concerns. Second, we will architect a modular service framework specifying data integration points (e.g., FHIR APIs), tiered alerting systems based on pre-2026 anomaly detection algorithms (e.g., isolation forests for passive sensor data), and defined clinician roles for PGHD review. Third, we will simulate the framework's performance and equity impacts using agent-based modeling calibrated with real-world telehealth utilization data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a concrete, empirically-grounded implementation blueprint for national telehealth services that meaningfully incorporates PGHD. It will advance the field of 'actionable sensing' by moving beyond detection to specify integrated socio-technical systems, and provide new insights into maintaining therapeutic empathy and equity in technology-mediated care at scale.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing Estrin's prior work on privacy interfaces, actionable sensing, and emotion in HCI into a pragmatic systems framework for national deployment, explicitly tackling the scale-up challenge that most mHealth research avoids and centering equity and clinician workflow from the outset.

proposals.title: Minimalist AR for Care: Evaluating Abstract vs. Photorealistic Visuals for Remote Patient Support Tasks
proposals.motivation: Augmented Reality (AR) is increasingly explored for remote clinical guidance and patient support, such as guiding a family caregiver through a wound dressing change. Pre-2026 AR research often assumes that higher visual fidelity and complexity lead to better user performance and understanding, driving resource-intensive development. This focus creates barriers to equitable, low-cost deployment in real-world health contexts where users may have limited technical literacy or use lower-end devices.
proposals.hypothesis: For structured, procedural remote guidance tasks in health and care, simple abstract AR annotations (e.g., arrows, highlights, wireframes) will be as effective as complex, photorealistic 3D overlays in supporting task completion accuracy and user confidence, while reducing cognitive load and system complexity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a within-subjects study where participants (e.g., clinicians, caregivers) perform guided physical tasks (e.g., assembling a medical device, locating items) via a tablet-based AR interface. Using a framework like ARKit/ARCore for environment tracking, we will compare two visual conditions: 1) Photorealistic 3D model overlays, and 2) minimalist annotations (2D arrows, bounding boxes, textual labels). We will measure task completion time, error rate, NASA-TLX cognitive load scores, and user-reported confidence and preference, analyzing differences with mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for 'minimalist AR' design principles, challenging the prevailing drive for visual complexity in health AR. It will contribute a framework for designing lightweight, accessible AR support tools that align with real-world constraints in telemedicine and patient-generated health data (PGHD) contexts, potentially increasing adoption for remote care.
proposals.novelty: While prior HCI work critiques technology complexity, few studies directly challenge the core assumption that visual fidelity is necessary for effective AR guidance in procedural health tasks. This proposal uniquely bridges Estrin's focus on actionable, clinically-supportive sensing with a critical examination of interface design dogma.

proposals.title: Patient-as-Primary-User AR: A Framework for Self-Guided Physical Exams with Clinician-in-the-Loop Oversight
proposals.motivation: The shift towards patient-generated health data (PGHD) and telemedicine, accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic, has exposed a gap in tools for structured, high-fidelity physical self-exams. Current video visits lack the clinician's guiding perspective, and passive sensing often misses intentional, protocol-driven assessments. This is critical for managing chronic conditions and post-operative care where frequent, in-person visits are burdensome.
proposals.hypothesis: An Augmented Reality (AR) interface designed explicitly for the patient as the primary user, providing real-time, context-aware guidance for self-examination, can produce clinically valid data and increase patient engagement and self-efficacy, while enabling effective remote oversight by clinicians.
proposals.method: We will develop an AR application for a consumer headset (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens 2) that guides patients through a standardized upper-body musculoskeletal exam. The system will use computer vision (OpenPose for pose estimation) to track patient movements and provide corrective feedback via spatial annotations. A clinician dashboard will receive a structured data log and key AR viewpoint snapshots, enabling asynchronous review. We will adapt 'Privacy Cards' methodology to co-design the interface with patients and clinicians, focusing on transparency of data capture and emotional display of the AR agent.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel human-computer interaction framework for 'actionable sensing' that transforms patients from data sources to active participants in clinical assessment. It will yield design principles for patient-primary AR in healthcare, specifically addressing privacy communication and the appropriate emotional tone of guidance systems, moving beyond detection to structured patient-clinician collaboration.
proposals.novelty: While most clinical AR research positions the clinician as the primary user, this work flips the paradigm, placing the patient in control of the AR view for self-guided protocol execution. It uniquely integrates Estrin's work on privacy mental models and the critique of emotional displays in HCI into the design of a proactive, patient-facing clinical tool.

proposals.title: Shared Frames: Applying Collaborative Gaming Mechanics to Augmented Reality Telementoring for Clinical Skill Development
proposals.motivation: Clinical telementoring via AR (e.g., for surgical or bedside procedures) often suffers from passive observation and misaligned situational awareness between mentor and trainee, limiting learning efficacy and patient safety. Drawing from pre-2026 work on patient-generated health data (PGHD) and actionable sensing, this project addresses the need for structured, engaging, and supportive remote collaboration that moves beyond simple video feed observation to active, guided co-participation.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding co-located multiplayer game mechanics—such as shared objectives, role-specific tasks, and real-time progress feedback—into an AR telementoring interface, we will increase mentor-trainee engagement, improve procedural task completion accuracy, and enhance the perceived supportiveness of the remote mentoring session compared to conventional AR video streaming.
proposals.method: We will develop an AR prototype using the Unity engine with AR Foundation for cross-platform deployment. The system will implement: 1) a shared 3D task ontology allowing mentors to place anchored, step-by-step holographic cues (e.g., arrows, highlights) visible in the trainee's view; 2) a collaborative progress tracker using a 'shared health bar' game mechanic that fills as sub-tasks are verified via mentor confirmation or simple computer vision analysis (e.g., OpenPose for posture); and 3) role-specific interaction modes (e.g., mentor: cue placement, trainee: cue acknowledgment) to structure turn-taking and reduce cognitive load. A within-subjects study with clinician pairs will compare this system to a baseline AR video feed for a simulated clinical task.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for 'actionable telepresence' in clinical training, translating abstract game design principles into concrete interaction patterns for high-stakes collaboration. It will generate empirical findings on how structured gamification affects engagement and performance in telemedicine contexts, extending Estrin's research on supportive, patient-centered sensing into the domain of clinician skill development.
proposals.novelty: While AR telementoring and serious games exist separately, this work uniquely integrates co-located multiplayer game mechanics—designed for shared physical space—into a distributed AR context to solve core problems of engagement and situational alignment, a synthesis not yet explored in pre-2026 clinical human-computer interaction research.

proposals.title: Bridging the Usability Chasm: Adapting Consumer Augmented Reality UX Methods for Clinical Passive Sensing Interfaces
proposals.motivation: The adoption of passive sensing and patient-generated health data (PGHD) in clinical care, especially for mental health, is hindered by complex interfaces that overwhelm novice users, including older adults and those with low digital literacy. This creates a 'usability chasm' that limits the clinical utility of these tools. While consumer AR applications have developed sophisticated, intuitive UX methods for onboarding and interaction, these have not been systematically adapted for clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: That UX research methods proven effective for onboarding and engagement in consumer AR applications can be systematically adapted and applied to significantly improve the usability, learnability, and perceived utility of clinical passive sensing interfaces for novice patient-users.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase, iterative design study. First, we will perform a comparative analysis of UX patterns in leading consumer AR apps (e.g., IKEA Place, Snapchat Lenses) and clinical PGHD dashboards. Second, we will adapt key methods—such as contextual onboarding tutorials, gesture-based progressive disclosure, and ambient status indicators—into prototypes for a clinical mental health sensing platform. Third, we will evaluate these prototypes using Estrin's 'Privacy Cards' methodology and think-aloud protocols with older adult participants and clinicians, measuring task completion time, error rates, and self-efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, validated framework for translating consumer-grade UX principles into clinical tool design, directly addressing the 'actionable sensing' gap. It will yield concrete design guidelines and prototype components that lower the barrier to entry for patients engaging with PGHD, thereby increasing adherence and data quality for clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: While UX research is common in both consumer tech and clinical informatics, this proposal uniquely posits that the specific interaction paradigms and onboarding strategies of *consumer AR*—designed for spatial, novice-user interaction—are a particularly apt and untapped resource for optimizing clinical sensing interfaces, moving beyond traditional GUI metaphors.

proposals.title: Scaling Down Care: A Framework for Asynchronous, Single-User AR Guidance to Bridge PGHD and Clinical Practice
proposals.motivation: Patient-generated health data (PGHD) and telemedicine have expanded access to care, but a critical gap remains in translating clinical advice into sustained, correct self-practice at home. This is especially acute in mental health interventions (e.g., exposure therapy, mindfulness) and physical rehabilitation, where synchronous clinician support is not always available. Current systems often lack the contextual, situated guidance needed for effective solo skill acquisition and review.
proposals.hypothesis: An augmented reality (AR) system that asynchronously overlays recorded expert guidance onto a user's local environment will improve the fidelity and adherence to prescribed therapeutic practices by making instructions spatially situated and personally reviewable, thereby enhancing the clinical actionability of PGHD.
proposals.method: We will develop a mobile AR framework using ARKit/ARCore for spatial anchoring. The system will allow clinicians to record short guidance videos, which are then processed to anchor virtual annotations (e.g., arrows, highlights, text) to specific, recognized objects or planes in the user's environment via SLAM-based mapping. For the user, the system will present these anchored recordings during solo practice sessions. We will integrate a lightweight logging mechanism to capture interaction metrics (e.g., task completion time, gaze patterns via front-facing camera) as a form of passive PGHD for clinician review.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes 1) a novel technical framework for creating and consuming asynchronous, spatially-anchored clinical guidance, and 2) empirical findings on how such 'scale-down' systems impact patient engagement, skill fidelity, and the clinician's ability to interpret PGHD within mental health and chronic care contexts. It advances the concept of actionable sensing by closing the loop between data collection and situated intervention.
proposals.novelty: While AR for remote expert guidance exists, prior work focuses on live, synchronous collaboration ("see what I see"). This proposal inverts the paradigm for asynchronous, single-user review and practice, directly addressing the challenge of scaling nuanced clinical support. It uniquely bridges Estrin's themes of passive sensing, PGHD, and clinical actionability within a care continuum.

proposals.title: Assessing Remote Mentoring Performance in Clinical Settings: An Augmented Reality Framework for Actionable Feedback
proposals.motivation: The rapid expansion of telemedicine and remote expert support, especially in clinical and mental healthcare, has shifted focus to upskilling local clinicians. However, the quality of this support depends critically on the mentoring skills of the remote expert, which are currently unmeasured. Poor remote mentoring can degrade care quality and learner outcomes, creating a critical gap in scalable, effective clinical training and support systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That an Augmented Reality (AR) system, instrumenting the local learner's environment, can passively capture multimodal data (gaze, gesture, verbal cues) to infer and quantify key dimensions of remote mentor performance, such as empathy, clarity, and attentiveness.
proposals.method: We will develop an AR application (e.g., on Microsoft HoloLens 2) for the local learner, which captures first-person video, audio, and eye gaze. Using computer vision (OpenPose for posture/gesture analysis) and NLP (prosody and sentiment analysis from pre-2026 toolkits), we will extract features of the local learner's state as proxies for mentor effectiveness. A rule-based and machine learning classifier (e.g., Random Forest) will map these features to mentor performance metrics derived from established clinical communication frameworks, providing real-time, private feedback to the remote mentor.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel technical framework for assessing, rather than just enabling, remote guidance. It translates principles from Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD) and actionable sensing into the domain of professional skill assessment, offering a tool for improving the quality and accountability of telemedicine and clinical mentorship.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the typical AR-for-guidance paradigm to assess the guide, not the guided, and applies passive, multi-modal sensing—common in patient health monitoring—to the novel problem of quantifying soft skills in remote clinical collaboration.

proposals.title: Dual-Use Augmented Reality for Patient-Generated Health Data: A Framework for Self-Care and Actionable Clinical Monitoring in Chronic Disease Management
proposals.motivation: The rise of patient-generated health data (PGHD) offers unprecedented opportunities for chronic disease management, yet a critical gap exists between passive sensing and actionable clinical insight. Current telemedicine and remote monitoring tools often burden patients with manual logging and fail to provide clinicians with context-rich, interpretable data for timely intervention, particularly in mental health and complex care regimens. This limits the potential for PGHD to support truly collaborative care.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-use Augmented Reality (AR) system that scaffolds patient self-care through contextual, in-situ guidance while simultaneously generating structured, interpretable adherence and behavioral data will improve patient engagement and provide clinicians with more actionable information for remote monitoring than current sensor-based or self-report methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a client-server system using Microsoft HoloLens 2 or similar AR glasses. The patient-facing client will employ computer vision (e.g., OpenPose for activity recognition, YOLO for object detection) to guide medication intake, physiotherapy exercises, or mood logging via ambient, voice-first interfaces. A local edge processing module will extract structured adherence metrics and behavioral features (e.g., exercise form, task completion time). This data, along with selective scene abstractions to preserve privacy, will be encrypted and transmitted to a provider dashboard. The dashboard will use simple trend analysis and anomaly detection algorithms (e.g., isolation forest) to flag significant deviations for clinician review, integrating with existing EHR systems via FHIR.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel technical framework for dual-use AR in healthcare, demonstrating how a single system can serve both assistive and observational functions. It will yield new design principles for creating clinically actionable PGHD streams from ambient interfaces and empirical findings on the privacy-utility trade-offs inherent in remote monitoring of detailed behavioral data.
proposals.novelty: While AR has been explored for patient guidance and sensors for remote monitoring, this proposal uniquely integrates them into a single, coherent system explicitly designed to generate structured clinical insights from passive, assistive interactions, moving "beyond detection" to support collaborative care decisions.

proposals.title: Minimalist AR for Accessible Telemedicine: A Symbol-and-Voice Interface to Bridge the Digital Health Literacy Divide
proposals.motivation: Telemedicine adoption is hindered by digital and health literacy barriers, particularly among older adults and underserved populations, exacerbating health disparities. Existing interfaces often increase cognitive load and anxiety, failing to support the 'passive' and 'care' aspects of patient-generated health data (PGHD) collection. This work addresses the critical need for interfaces that simplify, rather than complicate, the patient-clinician connection in remote care.
