proposals.hypothesis: A minimalist Augmented Reality (AR) interface, using a constrained set of universally recognizable symbols (e.g., ISO 7001) combined with conversational voice interaction, will reduce cognitive load, increase usability, and improve task completion rates for low-literacy populations in telemedicine consultations compared to standard video-call interfaces.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a prototype using the Microsoft HoloLens 2 (pre-2026 state) or smartphone-based ARCore/ARKit. The interface will present a fixed menu of 5-7 symbolic icons (e.g., heart, pill, question mark) for core telemedicine actions. Interaction will be primarily voice-first, using a constrained natural language processing (NLP) pipeline (e.g., Rasa or a rule-based dialog manager) to interpret simple commands and queries. We will evaluate the system through a mixed-methods study with older adults (n=30) with varying literacy levels, comparing task performance and perceived usability (via System Usability Scale) against a standard telemedicine app control.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, empirically validated interface paradigm for accessible telemedicine that operationalizes 'actionable sensing' by making PGHD contribution and clinical interaction simpler. It will yield design principles for 'scaling down' complexity in health technology, directly supporting clinicians by making remote patient engagement more effective for vulnerable populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores voice or AR for healthcare, this proposal uniquely combines a rigorously minimalist visual language (symbols) with voice in AR to create a dual-modality, low-cognitive-load interface specifically for low-literacy contexts, moving beyond detection to enable action. It applies Estrin's focus on privacy and mental models to a novel interface modality, asking how to surface clinical intent without complex menus or text.

proposals.title: Failure-Aware Adaptive AR: A Self-Stabilizing System for Care Tasks in Uncontrolled Home Environments
proposals.motivation: Augmented Reality (AR) holds promise for guiding low-literacy caregivers through complex home care tasks, but current systems fail in the messy, dynamic, and sensor-challenged environments where care actually occurs. This fragility undermines utility and trust, critical barriers identified in prior PGHD and telemedicine research. A system that cannot function reliably in real homes renders its designed simplicity moot for the intended users.
proposals.hypothesis: An AR system employing a failure-aware architecture that dynamically adapts its sensing and interaction modalities based on real-time environmental assessment will provide more continuous, reliable, and trustworthy support for care tasks than a static AR interface, thereby making the technology viable for low-literacy users in uncontrolled settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype using a sensor-fusion pipeline (RGB-D camera, IMU, optional sparse LIDAR) with a real-time scene understanding module based on a lightweight, pre-trained neural network (e.g., a MobileNet variant) to classify environmental 'failure modes' like poor lighting, clutter, or occlusion. A rule-based arbitrator, informed by this classification, will dynamically switch interaction modalities—for example, from visual overlay to audio-guided prompts or simplified graphical cues on a handheld device—to maintain task continuity. The system will log failure events and adaptations for iterative refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel system architecture and design principles for environmentally robust assistive technology, moving beyond detection to actionable support in clinical mental health and home care contexts. It will yield empirical findings on the relationship between system resilience and user trust for non-expert caregivers, advancing the field of actionable sensing.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior AR for care which assumes controlled conditions, this work centrally prioritizes and engineers for environmental failure as a first-class design constraint. The innovation lies in the closed-loop, self-stabilizing system that actively manages its own degradation to preserve core functionality, a significant departure from static or gracefully degrading interfaces.

proposals.title: The Caregiver's Field Guide: A Context-Aware, Low-Literacy AR Interface for In-Home Care Tasks
proposals.motivation: The deployment of AR for in-home caregiving is hindered by assumptions of high technical literacy, creating barriers for family caregivers and paraprofessionals. This limits the potential of AR to provide real-time, context-sensitive support for complex care tasks, a gap highlighted in research on actionable sensing for clinical mental healthcare. Drawing from the success of field guides in biology, which enable accurate identification with minimal prior knowledge, we propose a novel paradigm for AR design that prioritizes accessibility and reduces cognitive load.
proposals.hypothesis: An AR interface modeled on the principles of a naturalist's field guide—using environmental context to drive a step-by-step, visual dichotomous key—will enable caregivers with low technical literacy to perform complex care tasks more accurately and with greater confidence than using traditional AR interfaces or paper instructions.
proposals.method: We will develop an AR prototype using a Microsoft HoloLens 2, integrating its onboard RGB-D sensors for environmental and object recognition. A lightweight, on-device convolutional neural network (CNN) will classify care-related objects (e.g., medication bottles, wound dressings) and room contexts. Based on this classification, the system will trigger a pre-authored, branching 'dichotomous key' of visual overlays, presenting simple yes/no or A/B choices via minimalistic icons and arrows directly on the physical objects, guiding the user through the task without requiring menu navigation or text input.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel design framework for 'low-literacy AR,' shifting the focus from information display to guided action. It will produce a validated prototype and design principles for creating accessible, context-aware assistive technologies, directly advancing the goal of making PGHD collection and clinical protocol adherence more actionable for non-expert users in home settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses AR for instruction, this approach is novel in its direct analogy to biological field guides, explicitly rejecting complex UI paradigms in favor of a constrained, context-driven decision tree. It uniquely combines object recognition with a dichotomous key interaction model to minimize required literacy and prior knowledge.

proposals.title: Ambient AR Guidance for Caregivers: A Minimal-Literacy Interface for In-Home Patient Support
proposals.motivation: Augmented Reality (AR) holds promise for guiding on-site caregivers (e.g., family members, home health aides) in complex care tasks, but its adoption is hindered by assumptions requiring high technical literacy. This creates a digital divide, excluding those who could benefit most, particularly in mental health and chronic care support where timely, correct action is critical. Grounded in Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD) and telemedicine paradigms, this research addresses a key barrier to equitable, actionable sensing and support in real-world clinical care settings.
proposals.hypothesis: An AR interface designed with principles of passive sensing and minimal interaction literacy can enable caregivers with low technical proficiency to perform clinically-relevant support tasks as effectively as those with high technical literacy, thereby democratizing access to advanced care support tools.
proposals.method: We will design and evaluate a smartphone-based AR prototype using a Wizard-of-Oz methodology to simulate robust computer vision. The interface will employ context-aware, gesture-based interactions (e.g., point-to-select, hold-to-confirm) informed by pre-2026 work on ambient voice interfaces and privacy cards. It will integrate passively sensed PGHD (e.g., patient movement patterns, medication adherence signals) to trigger proactive, step-by-step AR visual overlays for tasks like medication prompting or de-escalation routines. Evaluation will involve a mixed-methods study with caregivers of older adults, comparing task completion, error rates, and perceived burden across technical literacy levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel design framework for 'minimal-literacy AR,' expanding the concept of accessible clinical decision support. It will produce empirical evidence on how to lower barriers for caregiver use of assistive technology, directly advancing the field towards actionable sensing in mental healthcare and beyond.
proposals.novelty: While prior AR in healthcare focuses on clinician training or patient education, this work uniquely targets the on-site caregiver's literacy challenge, merging Estrin's themes of passive sensing and PGHD with an explicit goal of designing for cognitive and technical accessibility, rather than assuming it.

proposals.title: Caregiver-Centric AR: An Actionable Sensing Framework for Autonomous In-Home Care Support
proposals.motivation: Current AR for healthcare focuses on remote expert guidance, creating dependency and failing to empower on-site caregivers, especially in mental health and chronic care where daily support is crucial. This is a critical gap in Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD) and telemedicine systems, which often burden clinicians with data review without providing real-time, actionable support to the person delivering care.
proposals.hypothesis: An AR interface designed to autonomously sense context, interpret PGHD, and deliver just-in-time, privacy-sensitive guidance will increase caregiver self-efficacy, reduce procedural anxiety, and improve adherence to care plans without requiring constant remote clinician oversight.
proposals.method: We will develop a smartphone/AR glasses prototype using a multi-modal sensing pipeline (computer vision for activity recognition, audio analysis for emotional valence, and integration of wearable PGHD streams). A lightweight, on-device inference engine, based on pre-2026 temporal convolutional networks, will map sensed context to a hierarchical care plan model. The AR interface will use spatial anchors to overlay context-aware cues (e.g., highlighting medication, displaying calming prompts during patient agitation) guided by principles from 'The Illusion of Empathy?' to avoid manipulative emotional displays.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel technical framework for 'actionable sensing' that translates passive data into autonomous caregiver support. It will yield design principles for caregiver-centric AR that prioritizes empowerment over remote surveillance, advancing the field beyond detection to intervention in mental health and chronic care at home.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the dominant AR-for-remote-expert paradigm, making the on-site caregiver the primary user of an autonomous guidance system. It uniquely integrates PGHD interpretation, real-time context sensing, and empathetic interface design into a single, actionable support tool.

proposals.title: Augmenting Clinical Encounters Now: A Software-Only AR Framework for Passive, Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD) Visualization on Ubiquitous Devices
proposals.motivation: Augmented Reality (AR) holds promise for making patient-generated health data (PGHD) from passive sensing more interpretable and actionable for clinicians and patients in mental healthcare. However, the field is stalled waiting for perfect, clinically-viable AR hardware. This delays the exploration of critical interaction models, privacy concerns, and clinical utility that could be investigated today using existing smartphones and tablets, which are already ubiquitous in care delivery and patient life.
proposals.hypothesis: A software-only AR framework, using marker-based tracking and sensor fusion on commodity devices, can effectively surface longitudinal PGHD in clinical settings to enhance shared decision-making and patient-clinician communication, without requiring specialized hardware.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype Android/iOS application using ARCore/ARKit and OpenCV for robust marker-based tracking (e.g., a clinic room object or printed card) to anchor PGHD visualizations. The framework will integrate passively collected data (e.g., mobility, sleep, communication patterns) from the Beiwe platform or similar, visualizing trends as temporally-anchored 3D graphs or icons in the clinician's physical space. We will adapt 'Privacy Card' methodologies to co-design visualization styles and interaction modes with clinicians and patients, focusing on mitigating the 'illusion of empathy' by ensuring data representations support genuine clinical insight.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a functional, evaluable prototype demonstrating immediate, low-cost AR for clinical PGHD, moving beyond detection to actionable visualization. It will yield design principles for AR-augmented clinical conversations and an empirical understanding of how software-only AR impacts perceived empathy, privacy, and utility in mental health settings.
proposals.novelty: While AR in healthcare often focuses on surgical guidance or training with high-end hardware, this proposal pivots to using software AR for psychosocial and communicative functions in routine care, explicitly decoupling AR utility from hardware advancement and grounding it in Estrin's themes of passive sensing, PGHD, and clinician support.

proposals.title: Low-Friction, AI-Guided Visual Capture for Patient-Generated Health Data in Routine Telemedicine
proposals.motivation: The rapid shift to telemedicine, accelerated pre-2026, is hampered by poor-quality visual data from patients, degrading diagnostic utility and clinician efficiency. Current solutions require dedicated apps or hardware, creating adoption barriers. This work addresses the need for passive, low-friction support that empowers patients to generate clinically useful visual data during asynchronous and synchronous virtual visits.
proposals.hypothesis: That a real-time, browser-based computer vision system providing immediate, simple visual feedback can significantly improve the technical quality (e.g., framing, lighting) and clinical adequacy of patient-captured visual exams, without increasing patient burden or requiring specialized software installation.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based system using TensorFlow.js or MediaPipe for client-side, real-time analysis of the smartphone camera feed. It will implement lightweight models (e.g., MobileNet variants) to classify common errors (off-angle camera, occlusion, poor lighting) and guide structured exams (e.g., throat visualization) via on-screen overlays. The system will be evaluated through a mixed-methods study with patients and clinicians, measuring objective image quality metrics and perceived utility.
proposals.contribution: This contributes a novel, deployable technical framework for improving PGHD capture at the point of generation. It advances actionable sensing in clinical care by translating detection into real-time guidance, and provides empirical evidence on the design of empathetic, supportive interfaces that reduce patient anxiety during remote clinical tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on telemedicine platforms or standalone assessment apps, this system uniquely focuses on AI-guided, in-the-moment correction within a standard web browser, eliminating installation friction. It applies principles from human-computer interaction and privacy-aware design to create a supportive, rather than purely diagnostic, AI agent for clinical settings.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Augmented Reality Integration in Telemedicine: Impacts on Clinical Workflow, Provider Burnout, and Team Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of telemedicine, accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic, has created new burdens on clinicians, including cognitive load, fragmented workflows, and a diminished sense of team cohesion, contributing to provider burnout. While Augmented Reality (AR) promises to enhance remote care by overlaying patient-generated health data (PGHD) and contextual information into the clinician's field of view, its long-term integration into clinical practice and its psychosocial impacts are unknown. This study addresses the critical gap between the technical potential of AR and its sustainable, human-centered adoption in real-world clinical mental healthcare settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the longitudinal integration of a privacy-sensitive AR interface for telemedicine will, over time, reduce clinician cognitive load and perceived burnout by streamlining access to PGHD and team communication, but that its adoption will reveal novel tensions in team dynamics and require evolving privacy norms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month mixed-methods field study with clinical teams providing tele-mental healthcare. Clinicians will use a custom AR headset (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens 2) running a sensing platform that surfaces relevant PGHD and patient history via ambient, glanceable displays during consultations. We will employ the 'Privacy Cards' method at intervals to surface evolving mental models and concerns. Quantitative metrics (workflow efficiency, self-reported burnout via Maslach Burnout Inventory) and qualitative data (ethnographic observation, structured interviews on team communication) will be collected longitudinally. Data analysis will combine statistical trend analysis with thematic coding of interview transcripts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a longitudinal, empirical understanding of how immersive technologies transform clinical practice beyond initial novelty, providing concrete design guidelines for sustainable AR integration in healthcare. It will advance the field of actionable clinical sensing by framing AR not just as a detection tool, but as a mediator of long-term clinician-patient and clinician-team relationships.
proposals.novelty: Existing research focuses on short-term AR feasibility or single-session usability; this study's novelty lies in its longitudinal lens on the socio-technical adaptation process, explicitly linking AR use to burnout and team dynamics—factors critical for scalable implementation but largely unexamined in prior work.

proposals.title: Crew Resource Management for Augmented Reality Telemedicine: A Framework for Shared Situation Awareness in Distributed Clinical Teams
proposals.motivation: The rapid expansion of telemedicine, accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic, has created new distributed care teams where a remote expert guides an on-site clinician or caregiver. This introduces critical communication and coordination challenges, risking errors and suboptimal care. Augmented Reality (AR) offers a promising medium for remote guidance but lacks structured protocols to ensure effective, safe team dynamics, a gap particularly acute in high-stakes mental health and procedural support scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting Aviation Crew Resource Management (CRM) principles—such as standardized communication, role clarity, and shared mental models—into the design of an AR telemedicine platform will measurably improve team situation awareness, reduce cognitive load, and increase procedural confidence compared to standard video-based telemedicine.
proposals.method: We will develop an AR prototype (using Microsoft HoloLens 2 SDK) that operationalizes CRM principles through specific interface modules: a persistent, gaze-aware 'role and task status' indicator, a structured verbal checklist system triggered by procedural milestones, and an AR overlay for annotating the physical environment with persistent, role-specific visual cues (e.g., 'Remote Lead Verified'). We will evaluate this system in a controlled study with clinician-caregiver dyads performing simulated wound care and agitation de-escalation tasks, measuring outcomes via validated situation awareness questionnaires, communication analysis, and workload scales (NASA-TLX).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, human-factors-grounded framework for designing AR clinical communication systems that prioritize team coordination over mere information transmission. It will produce actionable design patterns for embedding CRM into interactive health interfaces and empirical evidence on how structured protocols impact performance in distributed care, advancing the field beyond passive sensing towards actionable, team-oriented support.
proposals.novelty: While CRM training exists in healthcare, its direct translation into the core interaction design of an AR telemedicine system is novel. This moves beyond using AR as a simple visual pipe to actively structuring communication and role negotiation within the interface itself, addressing a key gap in clinical human-computer interaction for telemedicine.

proposals.title: Reducing Cognitive Load and Medical Error in High-Stress Telemedicine: A Human Factors Approach to Augmented Reality Interface Design
proposals.motivation: Telemedicine, especially for acute mental healthcare and remote patient monitoring, often occurs in high-stress, time-sensitive contexts where clinician cognitive load is high. Pre-2026 AR interfaces for tele-guidance or patient data visualization risk increasing this load with poorly integrated information, potentially leading to medical errors. Grounding AR design in human factors engineering is critical to make these interfaces supportive, not burdensome, for clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: That applying formal human factors methods (e.g., cognitive task analysis, ecological interface design) to the design of AR telemedicine interfaces will measurably reduce clinician cognitive load and procedural error rates during simulated high-stress remote care scenarios compared to conventional AR or 2D screen-based telemedicine interfaces.
proposals.method: We will conduct iterative, in-situ cognitive task analyses with clinicians in emergency and mental health telemedicine settings to identify critical decision points and information needs. Using this data, we will prototype AR interfaces (e.g., for Microsoft HoloLens 2) employing ecological interface design principles to create integrated, context-aware visualizations of patient-generated health data (PGHD) and procedural guidance. We will evaluate prototypes using a mixed-methods approach: quantitative measures of cognitive load (NASA-TLX, secondary task performance) and error rates during high-fidelity medical simulations, paired with qualitative analysis of clinician feedback using methods from Estrin's prior work on 'Privacy Cards' to surface mental models and interaction concerns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, human factors-grounded framework for AR interface design in clinical telemedicine, directly addressing the call for 'actionable sensing' in mental healthcare. It will contribute specific design guidelines and evaluation metrics for reducing cognitive load, moving beyond mere detection of stress to actively supporting clinician performance and patient safety in remote care.
proposals.novelty: While human factors is well-established in aviation and surgery, its systematic application to AR for *telemedicine* is nascent. This proposal uniquely integrates Estrin's expertise in PGHD, clinician support, and understanding user mental models to address the specific cognitive and emotional challenges of remote, high-stakes care, rather than focusing solely on technological feasibility.

proposals.title: Mastering the Interface: A Simulation-Based Mastery Learning Framework for Clinician Proficiency in Augmented Reality Mental Health Tools
proposals.motivation: Augmented Reality (AR) tools for clinical mental healthcare, such as exposure therapy aids or data visualization overlays, are emerging but risk poor clinical integration if providers are not proficient before patient use. Current training often relies on brief demonstrations, which fail to ensure competency and may lead to tool abandonment or patient harm. This gap is critical in mental health, where the therapeutic alliance and nuanced clinical judgment are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a simulation-based mastery learning (SBML) paradigm—where clinicians must meet a minimum passing standard on simulated AR clinical scenarios before certification—will lead to higher procedural proficiency, greater self-efficacy, and more appropriate clinical use of AR tools compared to conventional training methods.
proposals.method: We will develop an AR-SBML system using the Microsoft HoloLens 2 platform and Unity engine. The system will present clinicians with standardized patient scenarios (e.g., managing a patient in a panic attack with an AR biofeedback overlay). Performance will be assessed via integrated sensors (gaze tracking, gesture accuracy, task timing) and structured checklists. A mastery standard will be set using the Angoff method with an expert panel. The study will employ a randomized controlled trial comparing SBML to conventional video training, measuring outcomes through simulated performance scores, validated self-efficacy scales, and analysis of initial clinical deployment logs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, generalizable framework for competency-based AR training in healthcare, moving beyond simple detection of use to ensuring actionable skill. It will contribute specific design principles for creating effective clinical AR simulations that integrate assessment, aligning with the need for more rigorous evaluation of sensing and interface technologies in real-world care settings.
proposals.novelty: While SBML is established for procedural skills like surgery, its application to training for novel interactive computing interfaces like AR in mental health is unexplored. This bridges high-stakes clinical education methodology with human-computer interaction research, focusing on mastery of the tool-as-intervention rather than just its functionality.

proposals.title: Contingent Care: An Adaptive AR Interface for Clinical Workflow Support via Real-Time Multi-Modal Inference
proposals.motivation: Current clinical decision support systems are often rigid, interrupting workflows and failing to adapt to dynamic, high-stakes clinical environments. This is particularly problematic in mental healthcare, where telemedicine and in-person care require nuanced, context-aware support that respects clinician cognitive load and patient-specific contingencies. A system that can infer workflow state and adapt in real-time is needed to move from disruptive alerts to seamless, supportive augmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: An AR clinical support system that uses real-time, multi-modal sensing to infer clinician intent and cognitive load can dynamically configure information overlays to provide more effective, less disruptive support during both routine and unexpected workflow deviations.
proposals.method: We will develop a system integrating a multi-modal sensing suite (Microsoft HoloLens 2 for eye-tracking and scene understanding, inertial sensors for motion, and ambient audio) with real-time EHR context. A hierarchical hidden Markov model will fuse these streams to infer task phase, intent, and cognitive load. A contingent interface engine, built on principles of ecological interface design, will use this inference to dynamically render AR overlays (e.g., highlighting relevant lab values, suggesting next steps) via a rule-based system with reinforcement learning for personalization over time.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel framework for 'actionable sensing' in clinical settings, translating passive multi-modal data into adaptive interface actions. It advances the field of clinician-in-the-loop systems by demonstrating how AR can support, rather than disrupt, complex cognitive work, with direct implications for improving safety and efficiency in mental health and other clinical domains.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior AR clinical systems based on static task models, this approach dynamically infers workflow and cognitive state to contingently adapt support. It uniquely bridges Estrin's work on passive sensing for health with ecological interface design, creating a closed-loop system for real-time clinical augmentation.

proposals.title: Augmenting Routine Care: Using Cognitive Task Analysis to Design AR Workflow Support for Telemedicine
proposals.motivation: The rapid expansion of telemedicine, accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic, has created a need to scale high-quality, efficient care for routine visits. Clinicians face significant cognitive burdens in these virtual encounters, juggling patient data, visual assessment, and structured workflows across disparate digital interfaces. This inefficiency threatens visit quality and contributes to clinician burnout, yet most digital health tools focus on patient-facing support rather than augmenting the clinician's cognitive process.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying cognitive task analysis (CTA) to deconstruct routine telemedicine workflows will reveal specific, addressable cognitive bottlenecks. We hypothesize that designing augmented reality (AR) interfaces based on these CTA findings will reduce clinician cognitive load and task completion time while maintaining or improving the quality and consistency of assessments for common, low-acuity conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct in-depth CTA interviews and observations with primary care clinicians performing routine telemedicine visits (e.g., URIs, medication follow-ups). Using established CTA frameworks (e.g., the Critical Decision Method), we will model the workflow and identify key decision points and information needs. We will then prototype AR augmentations (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens 2) featuring dynamic, context-aware overlays of patient-generated health data (PGHD), automated SOAP note prompts, and visual guides for physical exam maneuvers observable via video. A within-subjects controlled experiment will evaluate the prototype against a standard video visit interface, measuring NASA-TLX cognitive load scores, visit duration, and assessment accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for applying CTA to the design of clinician-facing telemedicine systems. It will produce a validated, open-source model of cognitive demands in routine telemedicine and a proof-of-concept AR interface demonstrating measurable efficiency gains. This moves AR in healthcare beyond specialized surgical guidance into the high-volume domain of primary care, establishing it as a tool for cognitive support and workflow standardization.
proposals.novelty: While CTA is used in high-risk clinical settings and AR for complex procedures, their combined application to optimize the cognitive ergonomics of high-frequency, low-acuity telemedicine is novel. This approach shifts the focus from detecting patient states to directly augmenting and supporting the clinician's decision-making process in mundane, yet critical, virtual interactions.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Clinical Gaze: A Cognitive Task Analysis for Patient-Generated Health Data Integration via Augmented Reality
proposals.motivation: The rise of patient-generated health data (PGHD) from passive sensing offers immense potential for proactive mental healthcare, but it risks overwhelming clinicians with unstructured data streams. Current telemedicine and clinical support tools often fail to integrate this data seamlessly into existing clinical workflows, creating cognitive burden and limiting actionable insights. Augmented Reality (AR) presents a novel interface for contextual data presentation, but its design must be grounded in a deep understanding of clinical cognition to avoid disruptive illusions of empathy or usability pitfalls.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a formal Cognitive Task Analysis (CTA) to clinician workflows will reveal specific, high-leverage points where AR visualizations of PGHD and clinical data can reduce cognitive load, support decision-making, and enhance the therapeutic alliance in mental health care settings.
proposals.method: We will conduct in-depth, observation-based CTA interviews with psychiatrists and clinical psychologists (n=15-20) to map their diagnostic and monitoring workflows. Using the Critical Decision Method, we will identify key decision points, information needs, and potential errors. This decomposition will directly inform the design of a prototype AR system (developed on the Microsoft HoloLens 2 platform) that superimposes temporally-aligned PGHD streams (e.g., vocal biomarkers, activity logs) and electronic health record data onto the clinician's field of view during telemedicine sessions. The system's interface will be iteratively tested using think-aloud protocols to evaluate its impact on workflow efficiency and perceived support.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated cognitive model of clinical workflow for mental health, a novel AR interface architecture explicitly designed to reduce cognitive friction, and a methodological framework for applying CTA to the design of ambient clinical intelligence systems. It moves the field from 'detection' to 'actionable sensing' by providing a principled path for PGHD integration.
proposals.novelty: While CTA is established in high-stakes fields like aviation, its application to guide the design of AR for soft, relational data like PGHD in mental health is novel. It directly addresses the gap identified in 'Beyond Detection' by focusing on workflow integration, not just sensor accuracy, and grounds AR design in empirical models of clinician cognition to mitigate the 'illusion of empathy'.

proposals.title: AR-CARE: An Interoperable Augmented Reality Platform for Clinician-Guided, Patient-Generated Health Data Collection
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of single-purpose digital health tools creates fragmentation, increasing clinician cognitive load and impeding the integration of patient-generated health data (PGHD) into care. This is especially problematic in mental health and chronic care management, where actionable sensing must fit seamlessly into existing clinical workflows to be sustainable. A unified, low-cost platform is needed to host validated, task-specific applications without the overhead of multiple disparate systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular AR platform with interoperable task modules, co-designed with clinicians, will reduce tool fragmentation, lower adoption barriers, and enable more effective collection and contextualization of PGHD within clinical workflows compared to existing bespoke or monolithic systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a core AR software platform on commodity hardware (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens 2) providing shared services for calibration, display, and secure data streaming. The platform will host lightweight, single-task modules (e.g., for guided mindfulness exercises or pain self-reporting) defined via a clinician-facing authoring toolkit. We will employ participatory design with clinicians to develop modules, and conduct a mixed-methods deployment study in a telepsychiatry setting to evaluate workflow integration, usability, and privacy perceptions using methods from Estrin's prior work on 'Privacy Cards' and actionable sensing.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel technical architecture for interoperable clinical AR, a set of co-designed task modules for mental health support, and empirical findings on reducing fragmentation and supporting clinician-guided PGHD. It advances the field of actionable sensing by providing a scalable pathway from detection to integrated clinical action.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior AR systems built for single procedures or monolithic enterprise suites, this approach explicitly tackles fragmentation by combining the cost-benefit of single-purpose tools with the interoperability of a shared platform, centered on clinician workflow and PGHD integration rather than surgical precision.

proposals.title: The AR Probe: A Minimalist, Single-Task Augmented Reality System for Wound Measurement in Home Telemedicine
proposals.motivation: In clinical and home-based care, accurate wound assessment is critical for treatment but is often subjective and inconsistent, especially in remote settings. While general-purpose AR/VR platforms exist, they are complex, expensive, and face significant adoption barriers for clinicians and patients. There is a need for simple, trustworthy, and clinically actionable digital tools that integrate seamlessly into existing workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: A single-purpose, minimalist AR application for commodity smartphones, designed like a molecular probe to perform only wound measurement, will achieve higher clinical utility, user acceptance, and data quality than a multi-functional AR system by reducing complexity and aligning with specific clinical mental models.
proposals.method: We will develop a smartphone app using ARKit/Core ML (pre-2026 frameworks) that guides a user to capture a wound image with a reference object (e.g., a coin). The core algorithm will be a streamlined, on-device computer vision pipeline using a pre-trained U-Net model for wound segmentation, followed by planar homography to correct perspective and calculate precise area and dimensions. The interface will be co-designed with clinicians and patients via iterative prototyping, informed by methods from Estrin's prior work on privacy cards and emotion displays to surface user mental models and concerns.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, deployable tool for passive, patient-generated health data (PGHD) collection in wound care. It will provide a framework for designing 'AR probes'—minimalist, task-specific sensing systems—that bridge the gap between advanced sensing research and actionable clinical support, moving beyond detection to direct clinical utility.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the 'molecular probe' analogy to clinical AR, deliberately avoiding general-purpose functionality to optimize for a single task. This contrasts with prevailing AR research focused on expansive platforms, instead prioritizing adoption, interpretability, and integration into existing clinical and telemedicine practices.

proposals.title: Focused Augmented Reality for Patient-Generated Health Data: A Low-Cost, Clinician-Supported Tool for At-Home Wound Measurement
proposals.motivation: The adoption of AR in clinical care is hindered by high cost, complexity, and broad scope, limiting its utility for generating actionable Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD). Chronic wound management, a burdensome task requiring frequent clinical visits for measurement, exemplifies a need for simple, passive sensing tools that can support telemedicine and clinician decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: A single-purpose, smartphone-based AR tool for wound measurement, co-designed with clinicians and patients, can produce clinically valid PGHD, reduce measurement burden, and be adopted within existing clinical workflows more readily than general-purpose AR platforms.
proposals.method: We will develop a focused Android/iOS application using ARCore/ARKit for surface mapping and a color-thresholding segmentation algorithm to isolate wound boundaries from surrounding skin. The tool will guide users via simple audio/visual cues to ensure proper phone positioning. We will conduct iterative co-design workshops with wound care clinicians and older adult patients, informed by methods from Estrin's prior work on privacy cards and emotion displays, to create an intuitive, trust-supporting interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, open-source framework for developing 'minimalist' clinical AR tools. It will advance the field of actionable clinical sensing by demonstrating how scaling down technological ambition can increase real-world utility and support clinician-in-the-loop telemedicine models for chronic condition management.
proposals.novelty: Unlike research pursuing general clinical AR platforms, this proposal advocates for and tests a 'scale-down' paradigm, tightly coupling a single sensing task with clinician workflow and patient usability from the outset, moving beyond detection to integrated support.

proposals.title: Adaptive Evaluation Frameworks for Evolving Digital Mental Health Interventions: A Participatory, Multi-Fidelity Approach
proposals.motivation: Digital mental health tools, from passive sensing apps to telemedicine platforms, undergo frequent updates, rendering traditional, static evaluation methodologies obsolete. This creates a critical gap for clinicians and researchers who need to understand the real-world effectiveness and potential harms of these evolving interventions. Grounded in Patient-Generated Health Data (PGHD) and participatory design principles, this work addresses the urgent need for evaluation frameworks that keep pace with technological change.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive evaluation framework that integrates continuous, low-fidelity PGHD with periodic, high-fidelity clinical assessments will provide a more valid, actionable, and timely understanding of a digital tool's effectiveness as it evolves, compared to traditional pre-post study designs.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-fidelity evaluation architecture. The framework will employ adaptive time-series analysis (e.g., Bayesian structural time-series models) on streams of passive and self-reported PGHD to detect significant changes in user engagement and proximal outcomes following software updates. These continuous signals will trigger targeted, in-depth mixed-methods assessments (e.g., clinician interviews, Privacy Card exercises) co-designed with patients and clinicians to explore the 'why' behind the data, creating a closed-loop evaluation system.
proposals.contribution: This research will yield a novel methodological toolkit for the dynamic evaluation of clinical digital tools, moving 'beyond detection' to support actionable decision-making for developers and care teams. It will advance the science of PGHD utilization by formalizing how low-burden passive data can guide high-value, context-rich clinical inquiry.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on evaluating static tools or detecting events, this framework uniquely integrates continuous computational sensing with triggered participatory, clinical-grade evaluation, creating a living assessment model that evolves alongside the intervention itself.

ideas.title: Adaptive AR Clinical Support via Real-Time Workflow Inference and Contingent Interface Design
ideas.description: This research develops a system that moves beyond static cognitive task analysis to enable Augmented Reality (AR) interfaces that adapt to real-time clinical workflow dynamics. Instead of pre-defining an optimal integration path, the system uses a multi-modal sensing suite (e.g., eye-tracking, motion capture, ambient audio) combined with electronic health record context to infer clinician intent, cognitive load, and current task phase. An adaptive reasoning engine then dynamically configures AR information overlays and prompts, providing contingent support that aligns with the practitioner's immediate needs and the evolving clinical situation, thereby maintaining support efficacy during both routine and unexpected workflow deviations.

ideas.title: Cognitive Task Analysis for Deconstructing and Augmenting Routine Telemedicine Workflows
ideas.description: This research applies cognitive task analysis (CTA) to deconstruct the clinician's workflow during routine, low-acuity telemedicine visits, identifying specific cognitive bottlenecks and decision points. It then uses these CTA findings to design targeted AR augmentations—such as automated visual guides, integrated data overlays, and procedural checklists—that are seamlessly embedded into the clinician's field of view. The goal is to create an AR-supported system that minimizes extraneous cognitive effort, reduces visit duration, and standardizes quality for common assessments, thereby proving AR's value in scaling high-quality, efficient primary and urgent care. This moves AR beyond complex procedural guidance and establishes it as a fundamental tool for optimizing high-frequency clinical interactions.

ideas.title: AR as a 'Field Guide': Adapting Naturalist Observation Tools for Caregiver Support
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that on-site caregivers need high technical literacy for AR by drawing an analogy from field biology. In that field, the problem of enabling accurate species identification by non-experts was solved through context-aware, visual field guides and dichotomous keys that minimize required prior knowledge. Adapting this solution, the research proposes designing AR interfaces for caregivers that function like a 'field guide' for care tasks. The system would use environmental and object recognition to infer context and present information through an ultra-simple, step-by-step visual overlay, drastically reducing the need for technical literacy, navigation skills, or data entry from the on-site user.

ideas.title: AI-Guided, Low-Friction Visual Assessment for Routine Telemedicine
ideas.description: This research designs a system for routine telemedicine that uses real-time computer vision on the patient's standard smartphone camera feed to automate clinician guidance tasks, without requiring a dedicated AR application or headset. The system detects common patient errors (e.g., poor camera angle, inadequate lighting) and provides simple on-screen visual cues for correction, and can also guide the patient through structured visual exams (e.g., of a rash or throat). By focusing on AI-assisted guidance that works through a web browser, it minimizes adoption friction while objectively improving the quality and efficiency of visual data captured during low-acuity visits.

ideas.title: Environmentally Robust and Self-Stabilizing AR for Care Tasks in Uncontrolled Settings
ideas.description: This research designs an AR system that prioritizes environmental robustness and stability over mere interface simplicity for low-literacy caregivers. It focuses on creating a system that can self-calibrate and maintain functionality in the messy, unpredictable environments typical of home care, using adaptive sensor fusion and real-time scene understanding. The core innovation is a failure-aware architecture that ensures continuous, reliable support by dynamically switching interaction modalities when ideal AR conditions cannot be met. This approach ensures the system's utility and trustworthiness, making minimal technical literacy viable in practice, not just in theory.

ideas.title: A Modular, Single-Purpose AR Platform with Interoperable Task Modules
ideas.description: This research develops a unified, low-cost AR hardware and software platform designed to host a suite of focused, single-task applications. Instead of creating disparate tools, the system provides a core operating environment with shared calibration, display, and data services. Developers or clinicians can then deploy specific, validated task modules (e.g., for wound assessment, guided palpation, or surgical instrument tracking) that plug into this common platform. This approach retains the simplicity and cost benefits of single-purpose tools while solving the critical flaw of fragmentation, enabling seamless integration, reduced training overhead, and scalable adoption across clinical workflows.

ideas.title: Single-Purpose AR as a 'Molecular Probe' for Clinical Tasks
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from molecular biology, where complex systems are studied using highly specific, single-purpose molecular probes rather than attempting full-system imaging. The analogous solution is to design a focused 'AR probe'—a minimalist software application for a commodity smartphone that performs one clinical task, such as wound measurement. By stripping the AR system down to use only the essential sensors and algorithms required for that task (like camera, basic computer vision, and a reference object), the solution enables simpler, cheaper, and faster adoption in clinical settings, mirroring how a targeted probe enables precise study without a complex apparatus.

ideas.title: Adapt a stepped-wedge design from public health to evaluate the Limbr app in a real-world clinical setting
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from public health implementation science, this idea adapts the stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial design to evaluate the Limbr app for CLBP. In the analogous field, this design is used to roll out and test interventions across multiple sites (like clinics or communities) in a staged, randomized manner, allowing for robust evaluation while eventually providing the intervention to all control groups. The adapted research idea is to conduct a pragmatic RCT where primary care clinics are the clusters, randomly assigned to sequential crossover points from 'standard care' to 'standard care plus the Limbr app.' This design measures the app's effectiveness in a real-world clinical setting, controls for temporal trends, facilitates clinic-level implementation analysis, and encourages participation by guaranteeing all clinics will implement the app by the study's conclusion.

ideas.description: This research designs and validates a system that transforms raw patient-generated data from apps into clinically actionable information for EHR integration. The core innovation is a multi-stage processing pipeline that filters noise, applies clinical logic to detect significant deviations or trends, and generates succinct, prioritized alerts or summaries tailored to specific clinical contexts and workflows. The study will employ iterative co-design with clinicians and quantitative evaluation to ensure the system reduces cognitive load and improves decision quality, rather than simply adding more data to the electronic record.

ideas.title: Cloud-Anchored AR: Delivering Robust Experiences on Commodity Hardware via Hybrid Sensing
ideas.description: This research proposes a cloud-centric AR framework to overcome the sensor limitations of smartphones. The system pairs a lightweight mobile client with a powerful cloud backend that maintains persistent, shared 3D maps of environments. By matching the phone's live camera feed against these pre-existing dense maps, the system achieves stable, low-latency pose tracking and precise occlusion handling that far exceeds the capabilities of on-device SLAM. This approach enables compelling, multi-user AR experiences on today's devices by offloading the hardest perception problems to scalable infrastructure.

ideas.title: Minimal Viable Delivery: Testing SMS/Web Core Components via Ecological Interface Design Principles
ideas.description: This idea expands the original by drawing an analogy from Cognitive Systems Engineering, specifically Ecological Interface Design (EID). In complex human-machine systems, EID solves the problem of operator overload by designing interfaces that make the constraints and necessary actions for a specific domain directly perceivable, eliminating the need for complex, multi-function control suites. We adapt this solution by treating a dedicated mobile app suite as the 'complex control console' and SMS/lightweight web components as the 'ecological interfaces.' The research would involve deconstructing a proven multi-feature app intervention into its core functional components (e.g., daily symptom logging, psychoeducation delivery, action planning). Each component is then re-engineered as a maximally simple, context-independent unit delivered via SMS or a unique web link. The study tests the hypothesis that this minimalist, atomized delivery—which removes the overhead of app installation, updates, and navigation—can achieve similar user engagement and clinical outcomes by placing the 'knowledge' (the intervention) directly in the user's communication environment (SMS inbox or browser) rather than requiring it to be housed in a dedicated, managed application.

ideas.title: Adapting Aviation Data Bus Standards for App-to-EHR Data Integration
ideas.description: Future work should explore how app data integrates into clinical decision-making and electronic health records. Drawing an analogy from Aviation Systems Engineering, which solved integration of diverse subsystem data via standardized digital data bus protocols (e.g., ARINC 429), this research adapts that solution. It proposes the development and validation of a lightweight, standardized 'clinical data bus' protocol. This protocol would define message formats, metadata tagging (for source, timing, and data quality), and priority rules to structure app-generated data for reliable, interpretable, and actionable ingestion into EHR systems and clinical workflows, reducing the need for bespoke, point-to-point integrations.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Momentary Assessment and Behavioral Phenotyping to Predict Long-Term mHealth Adherence
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from behavioral ecology, where fine-grained movement data is used to phenotype animals and predict long-term outcomes. The analogous problem of predicting long-term engagement in a digital health app is solved by shifting from static patient factors to dynamic digital phenotyping. During the 3-month pilot, high-frequency engagement data (interaction logs, feature use sequences, temporal patterns) will be collected to construct distinct user behavioral phenotypes. The study will then investigate which of these digitally observed phenotypes serve as the strongest predictors of continued app use and therapeutic adherence over the long term (e.g., 12 months), offering a more nuanced and mechanistic understanding of engagement drivers than baseline characteristics alone.

ideas.title: Clinically-Triggered, Adaptive Self-Reporting for EHR Integration
ideas.description: This research investigates a novel adaptive self-reporting system where the frequency and content of patient self-reports are dynamically triggered by clinical context derived from the Electronic Health Record (EHR). Instead of fixed daily surveys, the system initiates brief, targeted self-reports based on clinical milestones (e.g., post-discharge, medication titration), scheduled appointments, or automated analysis of incoming EHR data (e.g., lab results, vital signs). This ensures the collected patient-reported data is maximally relevant for imminent clinical decisions and can be structured for direct integration into the EHR as discrete, actionable data points, fundamentally shifting self-reporting from a generic monitoring tool to a precision clinical instrument.

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Decision Support Systems for AI Coaching
ideas.description: This research seed expands by drawing an analogy from Clinical Medicine, specifically the development of Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS) which automated aspects of medical diagnosis and treatment. The analogous solution involved creating systems that integrate vast, structured knowledge bases with patient-specific data to generate evidence-based recommendations, proving capable of matching human performance in bounded domains. Adapted to the coaching context, the idea is to develop an AI Coaching Support System using a similar architecture: a core knowledge base of validated coaching frameworks and psychological principles, a module for continuous analysis of client data (verbal/written inputs, progress metrics), and an inference engine that generates tailored coaching actions, questions, and feedback. The research would rigorously test if this automated, systematic approach can match or exceed the efficacy of human coaching in achieving client-defined goals, thereby challenging the assumption that the nuanced support of coaching requires a human provider.

ideas.title: Ecological Validation of YADL: Linking Diverse Population Performance to Real-World Engagement and Adherence
ideas.description: This study proposes a longitudinal, multi-site investigation that concurrently validates the YADL functional status tool in broader, diverse CLBP populations and uses its repeated administration within a digital therapeutic platform to identify engagement predictors and long-term adherence. By embedding YADL within a 12-month intervention, the research will determine if the tool's sensitivity to change and baseline scores predict subsequent app engagement and adherence trajectories across different demographic and clinical subgroups. This approach tests the tool's real-world ecological validity and identifies which patient profiles, as characterized by YADL, are most likely to sustain engagement, thereby informing targeted implementation strategies.

ideas.title: Intrinsic Motivation Scaffolding through Gameful Feedback Systems
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond basic points and badges to design a system that surfaces and reinforces a user's intrinsic motivations. It employs game design elements like progress visualizations, narrative journeys, and social accountability partners to make the user's internal drivers and progress tangible. The system is designed to avoid controlling language and rewards, instead providing informational feedback and choice architecture that supports autonomy and mastery. The goal is to cultivate lasting engagement by aligning the app's structure with the user's pre-existing values and goals, making the activity itself more meaningful.

ideas.title: The Trust Scaffold: A Hybrid AI-Human Framework for Coaching Efficacy
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid coaching model where AI does not replace the human but systematically builds the preconditions for trust. The AI component acts as a preparatory 'scaffold,' using initial assessments, anonymized data analysis, and structured skill-building exercises to establish a baseline of safety and clarity for the client. This allows the human coach to enter the relationship at a more advanced stage of trust, focusing their irreplaceable human capacities on deep empathy, nuanced interpretation, and co-creating meaning. The system's efficacy is measured by the depth and speed of therapeutic alliance formation and client progress compared to purely human or purely AI-driven coaching.

ideas.title: Progressive Engagement Architecture with Just-in-Time Native Capabilities
ideas.description: This research tests a hybrid delivery system that begins all user interactions via lightweight, universal channels like SMS or a core web app to maximize initial reach and reduce friction. The system embeds intelligent triggers that monitor protocol requirements; when a step necessitates complex input, reliable offline functionality, or sustained background engagement, it automatically provisions a temporary, streamlined native environment (e.g., a dedicated micro-app or an enhanced PWA). This approach validates whether dynamically matching the delivery mechanism to the specific task complexity can achieve the high engagement and data fidelity of a full native suite, while maintaining the accessibility and low barrier to entry of the original lightweight concept.

ideas.title: Adaptive Calibration of Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) Sampling to Preserve Signal Fidelity
ideas.description: This research employs an adaptive, multi-phase trial to empirically derive the minimum effective sampling frequency for Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA). In an initial calibration phase, all participants provide high-frequency (e.g., 5x daily) self-reports to establish a person-specific ground truth time-series. In subsequent experimental phases, participants are randomized to various reduced schedules (e.g., random, time-contingent, or event-triggered), and the data from each schedule is rigorously tested for its ability to reconstruct the original signal and detect clinically or theoretically meaningful events. The final output is a validated, personalized sampling algorithm that minimizes burden while provably preserving measurement efficacy for the construct of interest.

ideas.title: Network-Agnostic AR through Decentralized Collective Spatial Intelligence
ideas.description: This research designs AR systems that operate entirely without dependency on traditional network infrastructure. Each device autonomously perceives and models its environment, storing data in a local spatial knowledge graph. When devices are co-located, they form secure, ad-hoc peer-to-peer meshes to share observations, resolve conflicts, and build a consensus model of the shared space, all performed on-device. This approach ensures robust, private, and functional AR in scenarios ranging from dense urban crowds to remote environments or during total network failure.

ideas.title: AR with Predictive Caching and Graceful Degradation, Inspired by Cellular Metabolism
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that effective AR requires consistently high-bandwidth, low-latency connections by drawing an analogy from systems biology, specifically cellular metabolism. Cells solve the problem of operating under highly variable energy/nutrient conditions through redundant pathways, energy storage, and mode-switching (e.g., aerobic vs. anaerobic respiration). Adapting this solution, we propose an AR system architecture with predictive caching (like glycogen/ATP storage) and a dual-mode rendering engine. A high-fidelity mode operates under good network conditions, while a robust, local 'graceful degradation' mode activates under poor connectivity, using pre-loaded assets and simplified processing to maintain core spatial awareness and information overlay. This ensures AR functionality and user experience remain intact despite intermittent or low-quality network conditions.

ideas.title: Applying an Ecological 'Keystone Species' Model to CLBP App Efficacy
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from Ecology. The original focus on discogenic pain alone parallels the historical conservation focus on single, charismatic species, which often failed to restore ecosystem health. The analogous solution was to identify and protect 'keystone species' or critical ecosystem functions that support the entire community. Adapted to CLBP app research, this means challenging the assumption that an app must be etiology-specific. The adapted research would design an intervention targeting a pivotal, cross-cutting psychological or behavioral mechanism (a 'keystone' factor like fear-avoidance) common to many CLBP subtypes. The study would then investigate if efficacy is achieved across a heterogeneous, non-specific CLBP population, thereby proving the value of a mechanism-focused, rather than a narrow etiology-focused, digital therapeutic approach.

ideas.title: Adapting Financial High-Frequency Trading Analytics for Dynamic Pain Modeling
ideas.description: This idea applies a solution from quantitative finance—specifically, the real-time, high-frequency analytics used in algorithmic trading—to the problem of dynamic pain modeling via Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA). In finance, high-frequency trading systems process continuous data streams to model fleeting, context-dependent patterns (e.g., volatility clusters, microstructural events) for immediate prediction and action. Adapted to the research context, EMA data streams (pain reports, activity from sensors, contextual prompts) are treated as high-frequency biobehavioral data. The analogous solution involves using time-series decomposition, models for volatility clustering (like GARCH), and event detection algorithms to identify dynamic, real-time relationships—such as how specific activities in specific contexts predict imminent pain flares. This enables the shift from static pain models to a dynamic, adaptive system capable of forecasting personal pain 'market' shifts and triggering tailored, just-in-time behavioral interventions.

ideas.title: Bias-Audit and Calibration of the YADL Tool for Equitable Measurement Across Diverse CLBP Populations
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple validation to conduct a rigorous audit and correction of measurement bias in the YADL tool. It employs a sequential mixed-methods design, first recruiting a large, stratified sample to statistically test for differential item functioning across key demographic and clinical subgroups. Subsequently, targeted qualitative work investigates the reasons for any identified bias, directly informing tool modification. The outcome is a calibrated, more equitable measurement instrument, along with explicit guidance on its context-specific use and interpretation, ensuring validity is not just claimed but demonstrably achieved across diverse populations.

ideas.title: Validate YADL tool using a multi-site, stratified sampling framework adapted from environmental biomonitoring
ideas.description: Further validation of the YADL functional status tool should move beyond simple expansion to a structured, generalizability-testing framework, analogous to how ecological indices are validated across diverse environments. In environmental biomonitoring, to validate a bio-indicator index, researchers use a multi-site, stratified sampling design across pre-defined environmental gradients. Adapting this solution, the YADL validation study would define population and setting strata (e.g., by age, socioeconomic status, clinical severity, care setting, and geography) and recruit a large, multi-site cohort that intentionally samples from each stratum. This approach systematically tests the tool's measurement properties across the full spectrum of the CLBP population, rigorously establishing its generalizability and identifying any context-specific limitations for clinical or research use.

ideas.title: Apply operant conditioning schedules from behavioral psychology to gamified app engagement
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from behavioral psychology—specifically operant conditioning schedules—to enhance gamification in apps. The analogous problem in psychology is how to sustain high rates of a desired behavior, solved by using variable-ratio reinforcement schedules (rewards after an unpredictable number of responses), which create powerful, persistent habits. The adapted research idea applies this by designing gamification elements (points, levels, rewards) to be delivered via psychologically-informed variable schedules rather than fixed, predictable patterns. This approach aims to leverage deeply rooted behavioral mechanisms to boost and sustain user engagement more effectively than conventional, static gamification systems.
